# Thread: overweight triathlete protocol
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overweight-triathlete-protocol.117/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### aintdoneyet — 2012-03-15T07:45:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overweight-triathlete-protocol.117/post-616>

I would like to start the protocol as best I can, I have access to a cols shower and the local temperature is sutable but I work 12 hour shifts, sometimes nights. I am a fit triathlete that needs to loose #80. I do have access to a lake for swimming but I wont be able to get to it every day, in fact for example I work 6 days in a row this week.

I would also like to add HCG to the protolcol but I do plan to eat more than the 500cal so I can keep training.

current health issues:

Hypothyroid (I take Aromour and decicated adreneal)

Hypogonadism: currently I have been 8 weeks without supplementation (I was injecting 2X per week)

Hypermotivated to getting the weight off and kicking some ass this year.

53 years old

I just got bloodwork drawn but no results back yet.

Doc what is the RX?

### Dextery — 2012-03-15T13:23:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overweight-triathlete-protocol.117/post-658>

> **aintdoneyet;617 wrote:** I would like to start the protocol as best I can, I have access to a cols shower and the local temperature is sutable but I work 12 hour shifts, sometimes nights. I am a fit triathlete that needs to loose #80. I do have access to a lake for swimming but I wont be able to get to it every day, in fact for example I work 6 days in a row this week.
>
> I would also like to add HCG to the protolcol but I do plan to eat more than the 500cal so I can keep training.
>
> current health issues:
>
> Hypothyroid (I take Aromour and decicated adreneal)
>
> Hypogonadism: currently I have been 8 weeks without supplementation (I was injecting 2X per week)
>
> Hypermotivated to getting the weight off and kicking some ass this year.
>
> 53 years old
>
> I just got bloodwork drawn but no results back yet.
>
> Doc what is the RX?

To say you are a fit triathlete that needs to lose eighty pounds is a bit of a stetch.

You need to read everyone of the Leptin Reset Protocol and everyone of the leaky gut syndrome protocol and the entire series on cold thermogenesis so you understand why you are not a fit person. If you marry all these of these protocols there is a chance you can become fit on the outside and more importantly fit on the inside.

Marathoners and triathletes all have a death rate that is far greater than the average American because the body is using up stem cells to replace cells that are damaged by servere training and competition. Also the rate of contracting diseases such as cancer is very high among this group.

You can get your telomeres tested at Spectracell Labs in Houston...mail...for $290 and you can compare your biological age to the age of your telomere length and I would bet the test would show your telomere length is much shorter than your biological age...meaning you have used up a huge amount of your stem cells for short term gain but in the long run a person that trains for and does endurance events will not have long longevity.

You can lengthen your telomeres, but you have to follow the three protocols absolutely to the letter

And you have to stop the training for triathalons, eat a ketogenic diet which means less than twenty carbs a day and turn yourself into a fat burning machine instead of a carb/sugar burning machine.

People are experiencing tremendous gains by following Dr Kruses to the letter and if you do you will enjoy a long healthy life without disease.

Dr Kruse has professional athletes following his protocols...especially the cold thermogenesis protocols and are seeing great results. He even has some 10 and 15 year old athletes who are running sprints faster than their peers by quarter of a second in a 50 yd dash.

It is all there in Dr kruse's writings for anyone willing to test his ideas without letting one's neolithic brain get in the way. And he has said there is much more coming.

Good luck with whatever path you take.

### Dextery — 2012-03-15T19:04:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overweight-triathlete-protocol.117/post-891>

This also about elite athletes that was posted on the CT6 comments:

Scary stuff from [www.strengthplanet.com](http://http://www.strengthplanet.com). I’ve seen similar stats elsewhere:

9\. What is the life expectancy of world class athletes?

Not so good. The average elite athlete will die by the age of 67. That is considerably lower then the 76 year life expectancy of the average American. Do you want to hear something that is really scary? According to the NFL Players Association, the average life expectancy of an NFL player is 58 years of age.

### Dan in Utah — 2012-03-17T01:16:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overweight-triathlete-protocol.117/post-1762>

Hey aintdoneyet,

I am a former triathlete. I got in to the sport in a vain attempt to get rid of my "spare tire." I put many hours in cycling, swimming, and oh yeah...running. Now I have an arthritic knee and back. Like Mark Sisson, I can't think back to a single event that I really enjoyed. Most of it was suffering. I was never any good at it anyway. I rarely finished in the top half of my age group.

I really found shangri-la with the paleo diet, leptin reset, and CT. **It's so much freakin' easier!** My tire is gone! My advice is to find a cheaper, funner, healthier alternative to the chronic-cardio.

Now I kick around the yard with the kids, hike a bit, throw big rocks and sticks, sprint a bit, play around with kettle-bells, and dive into whatever cold water I can find.

### SimonM — 2012-04-04T23:57:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overweight-triathlete-protocol.117/post-11492>

> **Dextery;659 wrote:** To say you are a fit triathlete that needs to lose eighty pounds is a bit of a stetch.
>
>
>
> You need to read everyone of the Leptin Reset Protocol and everyone of the leaky gut syndrome protocol and the entire series on cold thermogenesis so you understand why you are not a fit person. If you marry all these of these protocols there is a chance you can become fit on the outside and more importantly fit on the inside.
>
>
>
> Marathoners and triathletes all have a death rate that is far greater than the average American because the body is using up stem cells to replace cells that are damaged by servere training and competition. Also the rate of contracting diseases such as cancer is very high among this group.
>
>
>
> You can get your telomeres tested at Spectracell Labs in Houston...mail...for $290 and you can compare your biological age to the age of your telomere length and I would bet the test would show your telomere length is much shorter than your biological age...meaning you have used up a huge amount of your stem cells for short term gain but in the long run a person that trains for and does endurance events will not have long longevity.
>
>
>
> You can lengthen your telomeres, but you have to follow the three protocols absolutely to the letter
>
>
>
> And you have to stop the training for triathalons, eat a ketogenic diet which means less than twenty carbs a day and turn yourself into a fat burning machine instead of a carb/sugar burning machine.
>
>
>
> People are experiencing tremendous gains by following Dr Kruses to the letter and if you do you will enjoy a long healthy life without disease.
>
>
>
> Dr Kruse has professional athletes following his protocols...especially the cold thermogenesis protocols and are seeing great results. He even has some 10 and 15 year old athletes who are running sprints faster than their peers by quarter of a second in a 50 yd dash.
>
>
>
> It is all there in Dr kruse's writings for anyone willing to test his ideas without letting one's neolithic brain get in the way. And he has said there is much more coming.
>
>
>
> Good luck with whatever path you take.

A little harsh, Dextery!!!

Let's find out what aintdoneyet is actually doing. Most elite triathletes - and obviously we don't know whether this is true for our friend - ARE fat-burning machines. They train specifically to use fat as a primary fuel source rather than carbs, otherwise they would have to be continually eating throughout their events - triathlons take HOURS. And yes, they do use carb gels and drinks during the event.

Who are the professional athletes on his protocol? Where have you seen info about that? None have come forward, unless I've missed something. Armstrong and Phelps, often quoted, are not doing the protocol. As an athlete I'm not impressed by anecdotes about two kids, one of them apparently beating sprinters ten years older. I am super-sceptical. And before you let loose on me, I AM testing the ideas.

# Thread: MaggieC Primal the First
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/maggiec-primal-the-first.120/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MaggieC — 2012-03-15T08:51:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/maggiec-primal-the-first.120/post-628>

Wow, guess I'll get to open with the first journal!

I like what I've been reading and hearing about living primally. This is where I want to be. I lost quite a bit of weight on hCG, off and on for about a year and a half (down 50 lbs.) before I found Mark Sisson's blog. That lead me here, and I kept reading... and reading... P3 on the hCG diet is very similar to eating primally, and I was already comfortable with it, so I transitioned for about 2 weeks, and then I found the Leptin Reset.

I spent 6 weeks doing the Leptin Reset, and then I had to travel on a business trip. Last two days of the trip I ate carbs. I mean, I REALLY ATE CARBS. Two days back from that trip I'm still bloated and my legs are swollen. I also managed to put on about 7 - 8 lbs. in two days.

I thought that I would start to lose some weight near the end of the leptin reset, and instead, my weight was creeping upward. That's a very disheartening thing for someone who has trouble losing weight as much as I do. I had a basic battery of blood work done before I started, and now I need to have the tests done that Dr. Kruse tells us to do to see if that will give me any answers.

Exercise is difficult because I have joint issues in my hips, and tendonitis in my elbows and arthritis in my hands. I'm not even 50 years old. Been seeing an occupational therapist that tells me I need to accept my lot in life, it isn't going to get any better. Tomorrow, she gets fired, and I find another one. I am NOT settling. I think I can improve these issues, and by the heavens, I'm going to.

I'm an artist (painter) and a farmer with a fast growing farm/company that produces many things including Hawaiian coffee and macadamia nuts. I don't have time to be incapacitated, or even slowed. I'm riding the curve, and I need to be at my best.

So there ya go, an introduction, and a little background history. I look forward to talking with all of you!

Maggie

### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-15T10:02:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/maggiec-primal-the-first.120/post-636>

Thanks for going first, Maggie! Hang in there. Have you started with CT? Others have been reporting great gains re: joints and healing! Good luck.

### MaggieC — 2012-03-16T01:50:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/maggiec-primal-the-first.120/post-1160>

> **KiwiLauren;637 wrote:** Thanks for going first, Maggie! Hang in there. Have you started with CT? Others have been reporting great gains re: joints and healing! Good luck.

Thank you for responding! I don't even know what CT is. I would love to have a solution to these joint issues.

Today in my free time (ha ha...) I'm trying to figure out what I need to do for blood work. Obviously the straight LR didn't work for me although I had high hopes. Not a big surprise since I have asthma, hypothyroidism, adrenal fatigue, tanked hormones, tendonitis, and arthritis issues.

The only difference I noticed is that I "heat up" after meals now, and I'm not as cold as I used to be.

### Birdy — 2012-03-16T02:48:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/maggiec-primal-the-first.120/post-1197>

Some of the tests Dr. Kruse wants aren't listed on my sheet, so I need help getting them down on paper.

I already take ashwaganda as an adaptogen, I'll have to take more I guess.

### Hotmail — 2012-03-17T11:30:07+0200

Hey MaggieC, hope you don't mind me dropping in on your journal, re the exercise I understand, I had last year some joints pain, it came suddenly and disappeared suddenly, but it really scared me, how about swimming? I think it should be easier on the joints. And if the pool you select is cool, then you hit two birds in one stone, you get your exercise and your CT at the same time lol

Also when I had the joint pain last year, I noticed yoga really helps in relieving the pain.

All the best

### Phoenix — 2012-03-17T17:56:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/maggiec-primal-the-first.120/post-2022>

Hi MaggieC! I have osteoarthritis in my knees, and have been paleo for about 3 months. Remarkably, the crunchiness of one of my knees is almost gone. I've had a lot of crunchiness in it for decades. I think what's finally helping is completely cutting out gluten. And I mean completely. No cheating allowed for me. And last night in bed, after 20 mins in 55 degree water, my knee had this funny achy feel to it. This morning it feels almost normal. Not 100%, but definitely 95% better than it was. I consider this a miracle.

I'm beginning to think I can heal my arthritis this way. And that's not even why I started Paleo. I just wanted to do something about my weight. I need to lose about 30 lbs. Funny thing is that since I started Paleo I've only lost about 4. Other benefits have kept me motivated.

Keep up the good work!

Craving are still present but now I can resist where as before I couldn't.

# Thread: Keto Paleo for LRx and CT - Share your Great Food Ideas
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 39
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mumone — 2012-03-15T11:43:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-640>

Hey,

I did get Dr K's book and thats been great, however...

Im BORED stiff with what I'm eating and following a bad visit to my Doc, that ended in a block of chocolate  .... I need some good ideas for eating CO, staying below the carb limit, and inspiration for staying on track.

Please share your best ketosis meals

### primat — 2012-03-15T14:23:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-684>

Sushi with nori weed paper, using califlower "rice" instead of the regular stuff, mashed avocado and whatever seafoods you like- I use salmon and tuna.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-03-15T16:36:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-781>

I'm about to lose my mind with the number of eggs I've been eating in the mornings! I could use some BAB ideas!

### jeepifer@gmail.com — 2012-03-15T16:49:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-793>

Trout or salmon, pan-fried in coconut oil and lemon juice, with a side of sauteed spinach or kale.

Tuna and egg "salad" with homemade mayo, celery, onion, whatever you wish.

Ground beef cooked in coconut oil (or bacon grease, yum!) with 1/2 avocado and a few dashes of Frank's Red Hot sauce.

Shrimp and broccoli (or other veg of choice -- cabbage, spinach, cauliflower are pretty much my staples) with lemon and garlic.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-03-15T18:10:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-861>

> **primat;685 wrote:** Sushi with nori weed paper, using califlower "rice" instead of the regular stuff, mashed avocado and whatever seafoods you like- I use salmon and tuna.

I like this idea, thanks

### DarleenMB — 2012-03-15T23:34:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-1077>

JUlia Child's ground beef with herbs (sub in almond meal for the wheat flour) ... they are DEELICIOUS esp since I made 'em with a combo of butter and bacon grease. Recipe is in her "mastering the art of french cooking" cookbook.

You all realize that cookbooks written prior to the '50's are GREAT sources of inspiration for paleo recipes, don't you? You have to fiddle with them a tad bit to remove flour and undesirable ingredients but they are all "from scratch" recipes. Check out ebay for them.

### blindpig — 2012-03-16T00:29:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-1108>

> **DarleenMB;1078 wrote:**
>
> You all realize that cookbooks written prior to the '50's are GREAT sources of inspiration for paleo recipes, don't you? You have to fiddle with them a tad bit to remove flour and undesirable ingredients but they are all "from scratch" recipes. Check out ebay for them.

Gotta agree with this, Darleen. One of my go-to cookbooks for inspiration is the "Modern Encyclopedia of Cooking" from 1947. These old cookbooks usually have great information (and diagrams, too!) on how to dress, gut, prepare, etc. fish, game and other meats, for us modern folks who have grown up on cellophane wrapped packages from the grocery store. I'm probably not going to be fixing raccoon, turtle or woodchuck any time soon, but I love looking at these old recipes. I do have to stay away from the dessert and bread recipes, though. I encourage everyone to take Darleen's advice and check out Ebay for cookbooks!

(I can't help with specific great food ideas. My DH is having boring old grilled venison burgers for dinner. I don't eat dinner.)

### Evo Mama — 2012-03-16T01:07:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-1125>

I second the salmon idea. I stick it in the oven ( with butter and lemon juice underneath and on to of the fish) at 350, then jump in the shower for 20 min. It's done by the time I get out of the shower. 8oz. is about 50g of protein.

I'll also make meatballs: 2lbs. of groung beef, 1/3 cup parsley, 1/8 cup almond flour, 2 eggs, a bit of parm cheese (optional), 1/2-3/4 cup onion, a bit of garlic powder and oregano. They need to bake at 350 for about 40 min, so I make the ahead of time and reheat in the am.

I also prefer devilled eggs right now, made with homemade olive oil mayo (next time, I'll be making bacon mayo). They go down fast and easy, and the spice helps my taste buds from getting bored.

Gonna get some sausage at the farmers market this weekend, too. They' e got sage lamb sausage that is so yummy I could eat it every morning!

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-03-16T01:47:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-1158>

I am a crock pot cooking fool. My easiest recipe is to take any kind of meat (chicken, pork, beef) and put it in the pot with a jar or two of hot chunky salsa. Then I add my own favorite spices. Garlic, pepper and cumin. let it cook for 5-8 hours and you have tender spicy yumminess!!!!!!

The last pot i cooked I added bacon to it and it made great into optimal

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-03-16T03:46:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-1247>

I have to second the cauliflower "rice". Cauliflower is a great transition veggie - I make enchiladas with egg white tortillas and service with the cauli-rice cooked mexi style. Good enough for normal company.

Another great seafood option is sauted onions and garlic, add tomatoes whatever seasonings you like and toss in seafood. yummm.

Oh, my favorite for "on hand" is meatballs made with lots of spinach. I food process the spinach and add in ginormous quantities to meatballs. They stay all moist and yummy (and green).

Scotch eggs from MDA are great grab and go too.

I too suffer with the "oh god not another BAB". Been doing it awhile and still not hungry in the a.m. Any BAB suggestions welcome!

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-03-16T03:54:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-1251>

Meatballs and bacon in pumpkin soup. I just used canned pumpkin, water, seasoning like nutmeg, thyme, oregano, garlic, basil, turmeric, cinnamon, salt and pepper and add it all together. I love ground beef, but I really prefer ground turkey in this one. My favorite BAB!

Great idea on the homemade sushi. Where do I find sushi-grade fish though?

I love my bonebroth (I freeze bones in ziploc bag until I've collected enough). Then I add herbs like cilantro, parsley, basil and seaweed, often with meatballs.

Venison with cinnamon and salt. Then I mix coconut manna and coconut oil, with same seasonings, and dip the venison strips into the coconut mix. Sounds weird, but it's really good.

If you can get your hands on goose fat, it's to die for. I roasted a whole goose, and the fat on it tasted just like bacon. Divine!

Stuffed mushrooms with ground beef and spinach

The best way to not get bored is to invest in fun seasonings and make different combos. My go-to veggies (very few and far between though) are cauli-rice, pumpkin, mushrooms, kale and frozen spinach. Lately, my only carbs are coconut manna and coconut chips, however.

### Chrisgraff — 2012-03-16T18:20:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-1554>

Slow cooked pork shoulder is easy & really tasty!

1\. Liberally salt at least 2 hours before cooking (24 hours is best)

2\. Bring (closer) to room temp; typically 1.2 hours per pound. Keep meat covered until last 30 minutes.

3\. Using high heat, brown on stovetop in a heavy duty Dutch oven. Bacon fat is best, although macadamia oil works great too.

4\. Let meat rest on a plate for a moment, while placing quartered onions/carrots/celery/apple in the pot.

5\. \*Liberally\* sprinkle shoulder on all sides with dry rub. I like a combo of cocoa/cinnamon/chipotle powder/tumeric/garlic powder.

6\. Return to Dutch oven; fill pot with water or broth, covering the bottom 1/3 of the shoulder.

7\. Slice or two of bacon on top...(cover)...in to a 300 degree oven for two hours (or when center reaches 155 to 160 degrees).

8\. Let rest loosely covered with foil for 10-15 minutes.

This is really easy & delicious!

### tweety — 2012-03-16T21:50:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-1665>

Then I add my own favorite spices. Garlic, pepper and cumin. let it cook for 5-8 hours and you have tender spicy yumminess!!!!!!
>
>
>

+1 to the crockpot. I have two crock pots and at least one is cooking something everyday. Usually broths, but its always something. Then I can live my life instead of slaving in the kitchen!

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-03-17T14:00:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-1926>

> **tweety;1667 wrote:** +1 to the crockpot. I have two crock pots and at least one is cooking something everyday. Usually broths, but its always something. Then I can live my life instead of slaving in the kitchen!

That is funny I just bought a second one myself, so I can do big meats for several days and my smaller one when I am in the mood for something specific.

### meyoolia@gmail.com — 2012-03-17T23:39:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-2138>

My two favourites:

Ceviche (this should give you about 50g of protein)

\- 300g raw fish - I love salmon, but other fish work just as well

\- 4-5 limes (depending on juiciness)

\- 1+ shallot, minced

\- Salt and pepper

\- parsley or whatever herbs you prefer

chop up the fish, sprinkle with salt & pepper, cover witih lime juice. Mix in parsley. Let it sit, stirring occasionally, for 15 minutes or so. That's it. The lime juice acids will "cook" the fish.

CO

Squirrel Candy (a squirrel pilfered several of my paper muffin cupped first batch off the patio table, funny to watch it making off with a big white something, only to clue in later when i brought the remainders in that the little bugger had scarfed back about 6T of CO!)

\- 2c coconut oil

\- 1c coconut manna (coconut butter)

\- 1 dropperful of flavoured stevia in glycerine base (I love vanilla or hazelnut)

\- 1tsp each cinnamon, turmeric

\- go-in's: cocoa nibs (chopped up raw cocoa beans), toasted coconut flakes, citrus peel (my favourite is coarsely grated blood orange!), grated ginger

Melt oil & manna in a small pot at low heat (manna will burn if too hot!). Add stevia and spices. Stirring to keep mixture from settling, spoon into paper muffin cups, silicone muffin or mini-loaf "pans", sprinkle in desired go-ins and let cool til set.

Toasted coconut will give the squirrel candy a pleasant toothsomness, vaguely like rice krispies

### David — 2012-03-18T14:40:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-2353>

I now have two crock pots. I usually cook a free-range chicken from Trader Joe's in one of them. So very easy: wash the bird, salt and pepper it and sit it in the crock pot for 6-8 hours. No added water or anything, and it makes its own broth that can be used later for soup, etc. and the bones can be further cooked to make bone broth. My other, larger crock pot is used for roasts (I have a venison roast ready to go, at the moment.)

### NorCan — 2012-03-20T03:21:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-3070>

Salmon Cakes:

1 Can wild pink salmon (213 g or 7.6 oz) = 45.6 grams of protein

1 Egg = 12 grams of protein

Onion flakes or chopped green onion (optional)

Seasonings

Butter

Drain liquid from the can of salmon. In a bowl, mix the salmon, egg and onion flakes, along with whatever seasoning you like. Heat butter in a frying pan. Because the mix is so loose, the best you can do is place a spoonful of the mixture into the frying pan and form it into a patty with a spoon or fork. Normally, I can make four decent size patties. Once they have set a bit/browned, turn over and brown the other side. This is quick and easy...from the time you open the can, to the time you are ready to eat is roughly about 10 minutes. Once they’re plated, I slather them with butter and have a feast! For a total of 57.6 grams of protein and loads of fat...

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-03-20T03:24:06+0200

I have oxtail in my crockpot as I am typing this. Going to let it cook overnight and have it for breakfast.

### Cody — 2012-03-25T04:11:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-5655>

Doc is updating the cookbook for spring according to his Facebook. Cauliflower grits!!!

And chocolate pork ribs!

### TerrierMom — 2012-03-25T21:58:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-5972>

I had a surprise birthday party last night by a friend who LOVES to cook. She found out a week ago about my new way of eating and was hoping I would have a "cheat day" for our planned dinner (I didn't know it was a suprise party!).

No go, of course.... I am resetting my leptin, no cheating for me! No desire to either!

She pulled out ALL THE STOPS and had a seriously gourmet array of appetizer-like things that knocked my socks off, and I could eat about 75% of what she made!! I am getting the recipes and posting here.

My favorite thought was little cucumber rounds topped with smoked trout with a little horseradish mixed in, with some smelt eggs on top. DELISH. Recipes to follow soon, but, I have to tell you, she found some amazing stuff just looking through recipes online and knowing I would eat no dairy, grains, legumes, or sugar .....

Also I will recommend Dr K's Primal cookbook - some great ideas in there I am anxious to try!

### TerrierMom — 2012-04-06T15:56:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-12097>

Breakfast as of lately... Take chopped up pulled pork (from our smoker), warm in frying pan w coconut oil (smells heavenly) . Plate it, then top with a couple of pastured eggs (my whole foods has them... Taste way better than normal eggs !!) and half an avocado.

I haven't eaten lunch in 3 days due to this breakfast!!!

### Guest — 2012-04-10T01:48:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-13174>

> **Krusing\_tptimal\_in\_CA;12166 wrote:** Breakfast as of lately... Take chopped up pulled pork (from our smoker), warm in frying pan w coconut oil (smells heavenly) . Plate it, then top with a couple of pastured eggs (my whole foods has them... Taste way better than normal eggs !!) and half an avocado.
>
>
>
> I haven't eaten lunch in 3 days due to this breakfast!!!

Those pastured eggs at whole foods use to cost 6.99 and I thought that was bad and then they went up another dollar. I emailed them and complained (lately, I'm good at that) and they sent me 12 $1 coupons. Made me feel a little better. However, thank goodness I now I have a source from a local farmer that does not use SOY in his feed, where Vital Farm eggs does use soy. His eggs taste like butter and have the most colorful yokes I have ever seen. The only thing is he wants me to bake him grain free goodies, but he in return gives me pastured butter. I just have to keep my mouth away from what I make.

### freesia — 2012-04-10T02:11:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-13182>

Day 10 on LR here, and I've been having zero carb breakfasts. 50g protein, (fresh fish or beef) seasoning and fat. My time in the am. is limited so this has been suiting me fine, so far. I last 6 hours comfortably on this.

Moving forwards though I do want to make things more interesting so thanks for the great suggestions contributors!

Can anyone tell me whether bone broth counts at all towards the protein content? I noticed a couple of people mention they use commercial gelatin as a protein source in their BAB.

I've been making broth weekly for years (longtime Weston Price follower) and I always have chicken broth plus either lamb or beef broth in my freezer. This morning while I was making my way through my porterhouse I wondered if I added a cup of beef broth would that mean I could cut down an ounce or two of actual beef?

I guess...the best thing is to try it and see if my breakfast still passes my six hour test!

...But I just wondered if there was any offical word on it??

Anyone know?

Cheers.

### LinD — 2012-04-10T02:13:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-13184>

> **Chrisgraff;1556 wrote:** Slow cooked pork shoulder is easy & really tasty!
>
>
>
> 1\. Liberally salt at least 2 hours before cooking (24 hours is best)
>
>
>
> 2\. Bring (closer) to room temp; typically 1.2 hours per pound. Keep meat covered until last 30 minutes.
>
>
>
> 3\. Using high heat, brown on stovetop in a heavy duty Dutch oven. Bacon fat is best, although macadamia oil works great too.
>
>
>
> 4\. Let meat rest on a plate for a moment, while placing quartered onions/carrots/celery/apple in the pot.
>
>
>
> 5\. \*Liberally\* sprinkle shoulder on all sides with dry rub. I like a combo of cocoa/cinnamon/chipotle powder/tumeric/garlic powder.
>
>
>
> 6\. Return to Dutch oven; fill pot with water or broth, covering the bottom 1/3 of the shoulder.
>
>
>
> 7\. Slice or two of bacon on top...(cover)...in to a 300 degree oven for two hours (or when center reaches 155 to 160 degrees).
>
>
>
> 8\. Let rest loosely covered with foil for 10-15 minutes.
>
>
>
> This is really easy & delicious!

Looks great, Chris. Thanks !

I'll have to try this!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-10T04:48:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-13226>

Made an awesome Halibut today. Ground toasted macadamia nuts w, salt, pepper , dill and lime zest. Dredged halibut with egg white and coconut milk mix then coated with macadamia. Baked for 25 mins. SUPER DUPER YUMMY.

I call it Tropical lime halibut.

I also topped it with a sauce made in the same little foodprocesser (just left nut leftovers in it): Avocado, coconut milk, lime.

> **NorCan;3092 wrote:** Salmon Cakes:
>
>
>
> 1 Can wild pink salmon (213 g or 7.6 oz) = 45.6 grams of protein
>
> 1 Egg = 12 grams of protein
>
> Onion flakes or chopped green onion (optional)
>
> Seasonings
>
> Butter
>
>
>
> Drain liquid from the can of salmon. In a bowl, mix the salmon, egg and onion flakes, along with whatever seasoning you like. Heat butter in a frying pan. Because the mix is so loose, the best you can do is place a spoonful of the mixture into the frying pan and form it into a patty with a spoon or fork. Normally, I can make four decent size patties. Once they have set a bit/browned, turn over and brown the other side. This is quick and easy...from the time you open the can, to the time you are ready to eat is roughly about 10 minutes. Once theyâ€™re plated, I slather them with butter and have a feast! For a total of 57.6 grams of protein and loads of fat...

I love these too, nice and easy! But unless you are getting some crazy big eggs, I think they are 6g er egg, not 12. I wish they were 12, would make those BABs much easier!

my lunch today was not fancy but ...love it!

scrambled grassfed ground beef (a third pound or more) with chopped celery, garlic, onion, and from th backyard, 3 swiss chard leaves. Spices and herbs: oregano (fresh), chipotle powder, sea salt, pinch of stevia, apple cider vinegar (2 T.), cumin, with a liberal sprinkling of anise seeds!!!

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-04-24T03:56:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keto-paleo-for-lrx-and-ct-share-your-great-food-ideas.123/post-18245>

> **Trisha;782 wrote:** I'm about to lose my mind with the number of eggs I've been eating in the mornings! I could use some BAB ideas!

It's interesting, b/c sometimes I have to visit my parents. They eat 'SAD'. I buy organic/pastured eggs. Two things: There's a difference in taste, and second is that with regular eggs I don't feel full (also I'm limited to only two...). I get CRAVINGS for eggs, seafood, avacado. Like I miss all of this stuff...and this is only after two days, and I still stick pretty paleo even when at their place...it's just a lot harder and less (b/c I don't eat all the rice, pasta and potatoes: and am only allowed to have a small amount of meat 'b/c it's expensive' so you're supposed to eat the starch for 'filler': NO THANKS). LOL I sound like a whiner,,, but it's true. I DO help myself by buying grass fed butter and I sneak that into my food whenever I can. I bring my CO with me so that I can still have Bulletproof coffee and/or tea. I don't eat the starch and grains...and so helps if I indulge in a piece of cake (to not offend feelings). I always take extra vit. C in hopes that it regulates my liver extra. I will try activated charcoal too for occasions I need to eat bad for polite manners. (Tricks from Bulletproof. He also adds a betaine HCL with the vit C before the bad food/drink. Then activated charcoal 'after' the offensive food/drink).

I feel bad for being so...strict, but I prefer to eat how I feel best. Sorry...maybe have 'bad genes', but I sure know how to turn on 'good epigenetic' switches!

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-04-24T03:57:39+0300

And I make the coconut bark and sprinkle it with stevia (powdered)....mmmmm.

Ugh! I hate that about eating at my in-laws or someone else's house. There are so many extras -- starchy crap -- and the meat portions are so tiny! I always feel like a cow when I take more meat then the grown men, and the host looks at me like, how dare you eat so much of that expensive meat? (Maybe I'm imagining that thought haha!) But, I have found adding CO certainly helps to ward off the cravings and hunger that come when you're at the mercy of eating someone else's food.

When we stay in MN at my inlaws, I notice that even a non-paleo seasoning on meat gives me cravings and makes me feel off. I'm super sensitive these days, which makes me appreciate my boring approach to eating at home. It's simple, but it's satisfying!

### janagram — 2012-04-24T23:32:17+0300

Right, cowgirl!

But here's an easy delicious recipe I just had for lunch!

Cream of Artichoke soup. Artichoke bottoms from a can, drained, into the pan with a certain amount (not much) of chicken stock, and some CO and heavy cream....amounts you like. Canned artichokes can be briny, so no added sour stuff. I added curry spices, cayenne, and grated romano cheese, blended with stick blender!!! So good.

# Thread: CT without LRx diet??
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-without-lrx-diet.124/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ninja — 2012-03-15T11:57:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-without-lrx-diet.124/post-642>

My question is....**has anyone done the CT on either just a paleo diet or a SAD/CW diet?? and what are outcomes?**

As much as I want to jump right into everything right now, I'm on an extended vacation overseas, where food is part of the experience (when I stay in, I'm pretty good, almost always getting in my BAB in the morning), but the rest of the time, there is so much that I want to try, that it is impossible for me to follow Dr. K's protocol like I did when I was back home in Cali (I've tried multiple times already, always to get caught off guard with something tasty). CT might be the only thing that I'll be able to do for sure. I'll be going on a 12 day trip backpacking in Korea in a couple weeks, where I will (instead of staying at cozy hotels) spend my nights at their overnight spas (jjimjilbangs) where they have the cold tubs  The diet will be almost impossible for me to follow while I'm there, though. Wondering if it's worth it for me to spot-treat before going (since right now in Taiwan, I have no access to a tub).

### selma@decna.no — 2012-03-15T13:12:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-without-lrx-diet.124/post-654>

I'm not fully paleo / leptinRX. I do - let's say ... 70-80%?

I do CT on the same level. And it DOES good, but I suspect everything would work faster and better if I managed to do more (more completely paleo).

And I suspect that CT is better on a more optimal diet than on SAD ... because the body need good fuel to be able to tolerate the changes?

\*Thinking aloud\*

### Birdy — 2012-03-15T17:23:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-without-lrx-diet.124/post-815>

Dr. K says do the LR first, because you want your liver in top shape before the detox.

### hazyjane — 2012-03-15T20:20:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-without-lrx-diet.124/post-949>

I know that there are a lot of benefits of CT in general, but here's one thing to consider: it's a "biological mismatch" to do CT and eat a higher carb diet, and though there will still be some benefits, the ancient metabolic pathway (the melanocortin-leptin pathway) Dr. Kruse talks about isn't going to be activated.

So, you'll get some benefits, but not the full array of benefits

# Thread: Hi from all over SE Asia and San Diego!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-all-over-se-asia-and-san-diego.125/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ninja — 2012-03-15T12:13:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-all-over-se-asia-and-san-diego.125/post-644>

Hi!

I'm originally from Michigan, relocated to San Diego in 2007, and now on a 7 month trip (yay for vacations!) that will include Washington DC for a month, Taiwan for 3 months, South Korea for 12 days, Philippines for a week, and Beijing for 2 weeks. I stumbled upon Dr. Kruse's Leptin Reset/Prescription (LRx) back when I was tried HCG (lost a buncha weight, gained it all back, by the way). When I actually followed the LRx (about 3 weeks late last year), I felt FANTASTIC. All my aches and joint pain went away (huge for me, because I'm aware of a highly likely onset of arthritis because of a history of martial arts and a severe compression fracture in my L2 spine), my recovery from my sports was phenomenal, and I had al the energy in the world while doing my sports (brazilian jiu jitsu, mainly....staying away from my first love, taekwondo, because of my L2).

Once I get back to Cali (and away from all of the "once in a lifetime" tasty treats out here, I will be able to get back into the swing of things. Still trying to figure out how I can keep martial arts in my life while being optimal, if that's even possible. And if it's not possible to be optimal and practice martial arts, then how to be as close to optimal as I can with the martial arts (it's a must in my life).

I am open to questions, answers, new friends, and whatnot. I would love to have people to talk to about this, since my real life friends think I'm nuts

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-03-15T13:46:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-all-over-se-asia-and-san-diego.125/post-666>

Hey Ninja! I'm from South Dakota but was able to speak at a conference in San Diego late last year. I loved it! I'm so jealous of your world travels! Feed us some nuggets of your adventures once in awhile, will you? I don't post much on MDA, just lurk, but I always love what you add to the conversation. Happy to be here on Dr. K's site!

### Birdy — 2012-03-15T16:39:17+0200

# Thread: Howdy from South Dakota!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/howdy-from-south-dakota.126/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-03-15T12:16:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/howdy-from-south-dakota.126/post-645>

I've got to catch a plane, but I was so excited to see that this forum has been created, that I had to register and say hi!

I'm a rancher from South Dakota, and I freelance write to pay the bills. My highest weight was 177.7; with two rounds of hcg (started last March) couple with the Leptin Reset (keto-paleo started in September), along with CT (started in February), I'm still losing. I weigh 113.4 lbs; I'm in my 20s.

My biggest struggle is my adrenals, thyroid and hashis. I'm very hopeful Dr. K is going to help me turn things around.

I LOVE beef. It's my life. Seriously. If you ever have questions about it, or if you're interested in freezer beef (and live close), I would love to visit.

Grok on and stay cool, new friends! I'm so happy to be here!

### jeepifer@gmail.com — 2012-03-15T17:38:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/howdy-from-south-dakota.126/post-827>

Hey, neighbor! I love beef too, dontcha know!

### kc_boxerman@yahoo.com — 2012-03-15T21:37:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/howdy-from-south-dakota.126/post-1014>

Hey Paleocowgirl, thanks for dropping me a note - I'm your neighbor in Nebraska. I've just put a deposit on a side of (grassfinished) beef I expect to take delivery of this summer. I'm wondering if you have any recommendations for processing instructions - like a sample or something. This is my first time doing this - I know I want the goodies so I can render tallow, make bone broth, eat the heart (maybe make jerky of it?), but I'm not real experienced preparing any of this stuff. I know it will be dry aged and I'm going to request the better vacuum sealing...

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-03T03:57:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/howdy-from-south-dakota.126/post-10634>

> **Optim;1015 wrote:** Hey Paleocowgirl, thanks for dropping me a note - I'm your neighbor in Nebraska. I've just put a deposit on a side of (grassfinished) beef I expect to take delivery of this summer. I'm wondering if you have any recommendations for processing instructions - like a sample or something. This is my first time doing this - I know I want the goodies so I can render tallow, make bone broth, eat the heart (maybe make jerky of it?), but I'm not real experienced preparing any of this stuff. I know it will be dry aged and I'm going to request the better vacuum sealing...

Hey Optim,

Sorry I didn't see this earlier. I hope it's not too late to offer up some suggestions.

For processing, it's all based on preference. Personally, we like steaks much more than roasts, so we request that much of the chuck be cut into steaks (chuck steak is the end of the ribeye, so similar taste and fat, but the shape changes). We also don't request much hamburger. It's a common misconception that if you order more hamburger than steaks, you will save money. But, that takes the butcher time, so it's more costly. So, my advice is to keep most of it in steaks.

As far as the "extras," be sure to request he save things like the trim (fat), soup bones, liver, heart, tongue, short ribs, oxtail, etc. You can make jerky with heart, but I just like to slice it really thin and sautee it with CO, salt, pepper, garlic, turmeric and cumin with onions and mushrooms. Oxtail and short ribs are easy in the crockpot. For bone broth, I just keep all the extra bones we eat from steaks in a ziploc bag in the freezer until I have enough to make broth. Then just throw the bones in the crokpot with water, seasoning and a few T of apple cider vinegar and simmer for a day or two.

We go through beef fairly fast, so we don't worry about vacuum sealing -- the paper works well for us. I would skip that step if it will cost you more, jmho.

Good luck! Let me know if you have any other questions!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Structured (ie, can't choose the time) Workouts
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/structured-ie-cant-choose-the-time-workouts.127/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ninja — 2012-03-15T12:20:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/structured-ie-cant-choose-the-time-workouts.127/post-646>

I'm a martial arts lifer....been doing it since I was 8 years old (now 29). It's something that keeps me sane and happy, and I won't give it up. The only thing is...classes are typically offered at non-optimal times (6pm till around 10-11pm). Does anyone have any idea how I can structure my eating around my martial arts and still be (as close to) optimal?

### Shijin13 — 2012-03-15T14:43:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/structured-ie-cant-choose-the-time-workouts.127/post-696>

Eat before you go to class... so if class is at 7pm - eat before... or eat your BAB...then eat a BAL sometime btwn 2-4 and IF til your next BAB

there are days work precludes me from getting my work out in during an optimal time period...so I'll eat dinner, get the kids to bed and then do a 12min BodyRock.tv session at 930/10pm...then I'm in bed by 1030/11

We strive for optimal...but sometimes you work w/what you've got -that may mean it will take longer...but you'll get there...

### ninja — 2012-03-15T16:08:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/structured-ie-cant-choose-the-time-workouts.127/post-748>

Thanks! That's what I was doing before, so I guess I'll stick to that. I was worried about not getting in any protein after a workout, or working out so late in general....

# Thread: Warmup Time?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 28
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### meyoolia@gmail.com — 2012-03-15T13:14:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-656>

Hi all,

I'm up to 35 minutes in a 50F tub. QUESTION: how long does it take everyone to warm up again after an icy tub session? I'm wondering if I should spend less time in the water and go slower, cuz it takes me well over an hour to totally quit quivering  cheers, J

### primat — 2012-03-15T13:58:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-673>

After 30 min in 50F I get periodic shivers for about an hour afterwards. Core temp is super, skin temp is low.

### Evo Mama — 2012-03-15T14:19:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-682>

I've only been doing CT for a little over a week, with daily am cold showers, facial plunges and evening baths (30-40 min. at 50 degrees).

I shiver a lot while in the tub, but pretty much don't shiver when I get out. I feel cold, but I'm not shivering. My skin stays cold to the touch for at least 30 minutes after (I'm not sure beyond that because I'm asleep by then). After my first bath, I didn't put on any pants and went to bed with just a shirt on. In the morning, my legs were still cold to the touch. Ever since, I've been putting on sweat pants, and my legs warm up fast (warm to the touch by the time I fall asleep).

### BenG — 2012-03-15T14:26:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-686>

I did about 45 minutes in the tub. Was ok. Dryed off without clothes under the ceiling fan.. Was fine for another 20 minutes doing that. Didn't start feeling cold until I went under the covers.

### bennyha — 2012-03-15T14:34:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-690>

This is my problem as well. I get cold for the first 2 mins upon entering the water. Then I just listen to sports radio on my laptop set up next to me which distracts my mind from the cold. But after I get out after 35 mins I am brutal cold and shiver for well up to an hour. This first day I did it I actually shivered for 2 hours straight after word and felt miserable. It's slowly getting better. But the hour after getting out of the cold tub is much worse than actually sitting in the cold tub for me

### primat — 2012-03-15T14:48:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-698>

> **bennyha;691 wrote:** It's slowly getting better. But the hour after getting out of the cold tub is much worse than actually sitting in the cold tub for me

Ditto. I'm thinking of dialing it back a notch and do shorter sessions at the same (50-55f) temp.

### bennyha — 2012-03-15T14:52:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-699>

> **primat;699 wrote:** Ditto. I'm thinking of dialing it back a notch and do shorter sessions at the same (50-55f) temp.

I've been thinking of doing the same thing. I'm hoping my body will cope better after the sessions if maybe I lessen the duration of the tub a bit and let my body gradually adapt to the cold

### meyoolia@gmail.com — 2012-03-15T17:41:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-829>

> **bennyha;691 wrote:** This is my problem as well. I get cold for the first 2 mins upon entering the water. Then I just listen to sports radio on my laptop set up next to me which distracts my mind from the cold. But after I get out after 35 mins I am brutal cold and shiver for well up to an hour. This first day I did it I actually shivered for 2 hours straight after word and felt miserable. It's slowly getting better. But the hour after getting out of the cold tub is much worse than actually sitting in the cold tub for me

EXACTLY! I could hang out for the full 40 minutes and more without any distress....but 5-10 minutes out and YIKES! freezing for another hour or two or... after that

On the CT6 blog thread, vwaggs said "For those of you having a tough time with the baths and shivering for hours, here is my experience. I am not sick and only have about 5 more lbs to lose. I started the baths and did them every other night for 25-30 min.(just couldnÃ¢â‚¬â„¢t get myself to do it every night but now I am wondering if this would have been faster). I have been doing this for 3 weeks and finally last night I actually felt pretty comfortable in the bath (still shivered but it wasnÃ¢â‚¬â„¢t horrible like before). When I got out, about 10 min. later my furnace kicked on. The top part of my back started to heat up and my daughter said when she touched it, it felt fever hot. It felt fabulous! Then the rest of me warmed up fast too! I lost 1 lb overnight (which is a lot since I donÃ¢â‚¬â„¢t have much left to lose). My water has been about 55 degrees and I put ice packs on my bottom and thighs (belly is flat). The skin temp gets to 50 with ice and the rest of my skin is still in the 60′s where the ice doesnÃ¢â‚¬â„¢t hit. May make ice blocks for todayÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s bath. The BEST part of this is my lack of appetite! I no longer need a crazy big BAB to get through the day." So... looks like there IS hope if we "stay the course" ...?

### MartiD — 2012-03-15T17:50:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-839>

My recovery time is definately improving each day. I was having much harder time in the evenings when I started 3 weeks about but in the last two days it has been much easier and I am warmed up within a 45 minute time frame. In the mornings I hardly even think about it after I get out because I am hurry around getting ready for work. I do have crazy energy for about an hour after I get though.

I thought this whole process was crazy when I started, how I look forward to it twice a day! Is that crazy or what????

### Lyndra — 2012-03-15T20:00:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-931>

I've been doing cold baths for 9 days now and am fine in the bath but ridiculously cold for an hour afterwards. I'm hoping that soon I will turn on the pilot light and warm up faster.

### mycrazycartoons@gmail.com — 2012-03-15T20:06:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-934>

Haha.. I thought the same thing.. my family thinks I'm nuts--I'm literally cold to the touch right now.. with the cold tub and then a chill vest ... shivering cold. Also waiting for my pilot light to warm up. (challenging to type with cold fingers)

### d_grhrt@yahoo.com — 2012-03-20T04:44:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-3111>

I'm 1&1/2 weeks in. Started the tub after 4 days because the face dunking was too easy. Pilot light has not yet turned on so my shivering kicks in within 15 mins after I get out and lasts about an hour. I'm now doing morning and evening 35-45 mins, 53 degrees (my tap's temp) with ice packs on stomach.

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-08T23:38:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-12792>

I have found that heating a large rice pack in the oven (275) and putting it over my torso heats me up best after the ice bath. Trying to heat up my extremeties first seems to make the shivering worse. Just remember that your body wants to keep your heart/torso warm first, so by providing heat to that area your body can afford to send warm blood to other areas.

### Adria — 2012-04-09T00:36:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-12806>

I'm not letting myself shiver. By going slow my body has been heating itself up about 20min after the bath, I can feel the heat radiating. I will only stay in the tub 15-20min max, this is working for me.

### joyfuljoyful — 2012-04-09T01:30:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-12818>

Ok, here is a secret! STAY NAKED AFTER THE TUB! I wish I could remember who posted this tip and where it is on the forum. She had tried it and had a much easier time warming up. I too DREADED the shakes and shivers after the bath (definitely worse than the bath itself!) but then I implemented this advice. Now I just head to my room, and do some squats and a few pushups, maybe some planks, (more to distract myself from the cold) and I kid you not: I am comfortably warm (but skin still cool to the touch, but absolutely no shivers or shakes, or discomfort) within 10 to 15 min max! Try it. Now the worst part is putting my robe on to make the walk from the bathroom to my bedroom (my kids and their friends dont need to see that! ha) Then when I take the robe off, I immediately feel better.

### trish3 — 2012-04-09T04:42:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-12881>

I think to try the naked thing might help.

Does anyone recall whether we WANT to stay cold? Or is it okay to get warmed up? Also, does it matter if I just submerge my lower half? That is quite easy, and I would prefer to keep my 'frontage' intact lol

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-09T04:45:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-12883>

> **joyfuljoyful;12887 wrote:** Ok, here is a secret! STAY NAKED AFTER THE TUB! I wish I could remember who posted this tip and where it is on the forum. She had tried it and had a much easier time warming up. I too DREADED the shakes and shivers after the bath (definitely worse than the bath itself!) but then I implemented this advice. Now I just head to my room, and do some squats and a few pushups, maybe some planks, (more to distract myself from the cold) and I kid you not: I am comfortably warm (but skin still cool to the touch, but absolutely no shivers or shakes, or discomfort) within 10 to 15 min max! Try it. Now the worst part is putting my robe on to make the walk from the bathroom to my bedroom (my kids and their friends dont need to see that! ha) Then when I take the robe off, I immediately feel better.

I definitely agree with this. I put on light pj's only, no socks etc. and avoid getting under the bed covers until I'm warm. All the clothes and blankets seem to hold the cold in.

I also meant to add on my last post about a huge mistake I made. I was tired of waiting to warm up, so after my cold bath I put hot water in the tub. It made me shiver and shake terribly and I was WAY colder than before and both in the hot water and then later out of the water I took SO much longer to warm up. The other thing was the water was really hot according to my instant read therm. (although I've been questioning it's accuracy by maybe even 10 deg.), I usually can't get into a hot tub that is 102 yet it read 110 and it felt luke warm. I don't know if it was me, or the water really was cooler than it said, but I had turned it almost straight hot and our water heater is set to 120 so that does hint at the therm. being correct. Anyhoo, DON"T do THAT!!

### shilohman — 2012-04-09T04:57:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-12888>

> **bennyha;700 wrote:** I've been thinking of doing the same thing. I'm hoping my body will cope better after the sessions if maybe I lessen the duration of the tub a bit and let my body gradually adapt to the cold

That is exactly what I did and now I have zero warm up time after CT.

### joyfuljoyful — 2012-04-09T06:27:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-12915>

> **trish3;12950 wrote:** I think to try the naked thing might help.
>
> Does anyone recall whether we WANT to stay cold? Or is it okay to get warmed up? Also, does it matter if I just submerge my lower half? That is quite easy, and I would prefer to keep my 'frontage' intact lol

I recline in the tub, learning back on Ice packs, and my lower half and belly are submerged, but the girls stay out. I lower my upper arms into the water. My understanding is that every bit helps. Once a step got easier, I would submerge more and apply more Ice packs.

I try to not do anything artificial, per se, to warm up. I have done it but try not to. I say everyone really needs to stay naked after the tub, it makes it sooo much easier. Also for me it is easier to do ct earlier in the day, I had a harder time warming up when I tried it before bed. Also it makes a huge difference to have food in your belly , and the more fat the better.

### caroline — 2012-04-09T06:48:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-12916>

I so agree! naked is the way to go! never, ever thought I would say that! I think I have missed / forgot about this high fat thing before C/T . What do you eat before? So we should be upping the fat and consuming moderate protein - right? can you give me an example please?

### agatha — 2012-04-09T13:50:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-12936>

> **PaleoMom;12952 wrote:** I definitely agree with this. I put on light pj's only, no socks etc. and avoid getting under the bed covers until I'm warm. All the clothes and blankets seem to hold the cold in.
>
>
>
> I also meant to add on my last post about a huge mistake I made. I was tired of waiting to warm up, so after my cold bath I put hot water in the tub. It made me shiver and shake terribly and I was WAY colder than before and both in the hot water and then later out of the water I took SO much longer to warm up. The other thing was the water was really hot according to my instant read therm. (although I've been questioning it's accuracy by maybe even 10 deg.), I usually can't get into a hot tub that is 102 yet it read 110 and it felt luke warm. I don't know if it was me, or the water really was cooler than it said, but I had turned it almost straight hot and our water heater is set to 120 so that does hint at the therm. being correct. Anyhoo, DON"T do THAT!!

Yes, if you artifically warm the periphery whilst warming up it will pull blood away from the core and you will feel very very very cold. I have found this, even holding a hot drink after CT. Naked is definitely better.

There is a fabulous article linked by KiwiLauren on page 2 of the What body parts are you prioritising thread. (sorry don't know how to paste it here) - it mentions that the UCP1 (which warms us up in non shivering thermogenesis) takes a few weeks to be produced in quantity so we need to give ourselves a few weeks to adapt before the shivering will lessen. I think this is probably what Jack means by the pilot light switching on. Presumably we need to allow ourselves to shiver a little as this will then tell the body that our current levels of BAT/UCP1 are insufficient for the environment and it needs to generate more.

### MonteD — 2012-04-11T07:42:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-13663>

I thought we were aiming to **not** shiver _during_ the CT sessions? Isn't that a much different form of thermogenesis (muscle contractions). BAT formation occurs above shivering. Just wondering...

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-04-11T10:19:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-13670>

I took bitter melon for the first time yesterday prior to a cold bath. For the first time yesterday, I felt initially very cold (although not as cold as normal), and then almost burning hot in the bath. My warm-up time afterwards was much shorter than it has been - fifteen to twenty minutes as opposed to a couple of hours. But I would say that the shivering involved was much more intense, even though it was for a shorter time. Very strange feeling, and wondering what today will bring.

### vwaggs — 2012-04-18T04:40:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-16117>

I also agree with less clothes after CT! I keep really busy after and I warm up super fast and only shiver for 5 min with a t-shirt and shorts. When I put on tons of clothes and got under the covers, it took me hours to warm up. A few (3 sets of 10) pushups help warm up the "inside" while my skin stays comfortably cold which I think is the goal. Plus when I had detox symptoms (rash, cough, and sinus junk) it was super hard to CT and to warm up. Much easier now that the detox is over. Did that happen to anyone else?

### Destiny — 2012-04-18T13:13:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-16176>

> **bennyha;691 wrote:** This is my problem as well. I get cold for the first 2 mins upon entering the water. Then I just listen to sports radio on my laptop set up next to me which distracts my mind from the cold. But after I get out after 35 mins I am brutal cold and shiver for well up to an hour. This first day I did it I actually shivered for 2 hours straight after word and felt miserable. It's slowly getting better. But the hour after getting out of the cold tub is much worse than actually sitting in the cold tub for me

Do you wear a hat? It really helped me with the cold upon entering the water. I also had another small towel around my neck. Later, I just took them off.

### vwaggs — 2012-04-18T18:04:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-16224>

> **Anna K.;16198 wrote:** I really didn't have much of detox, just "sinus junk" for a few days. But it still takes me a long time to warm up and I've been doing it for about 4 weeks.
>
>
>
> How long did you detox last and how long you are in the bath at what Temp? thanks.

I detoxed for 3 solid weeks and it took about 3 weeks before I started even detoxing. Baths were very painful during that time and my appetite which was nothing the first 3 weeks, increased during the detox. I took it a bit slower with the baths when I felt horrible. I stay in the bath for 25-30 min every other day. Temp started around 50-55 in March and is now up to 60-63 even with tons of ice. I also use ice under a compression garment to get the skin temp down to 50-55. I use the ice on my butt, thighs and back. Even though I haven't lost much weight (I am close to goal after doing hCG) the cellulite is going away which is very exciting. So now 7-8 weeks in, things are getting easier as the detox symptoms are almost gone. I shiver for the first 3 minutes in and last 3 minutes before I get out. (I do shiver a little in between there but nothing like the first few baths).

### vkiernan — 2012-04-18T21:09:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-16310>

> **Anna K.;16374 wrote:** I've also noticed that. I didn't have much fat to lose , but i did have a little bit of cellulite and now most of it is gone, amazing.

Cellulite going away? Hmmm, I sure hope this works over time for me. I did just lose a little weight but I haven't noticed cellulite budging. I don't have weight to lost but I do want to swap muscle pounds for fat pounds - you know, bad body comp, skinny fat!

### vwaggs — 2012-04-19T03:46:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/warmup-time.131/post-16428>

> **Anna K.;16377 wrote:** thanks for your response. Do you shiver after you get out? How long does it take you to warm back up?

Yes I do but only for 5 min. or so and then I am fine. I still feel cold for awhile but not uncomfortable. I used to shiver for hours after. My back starts to heat up about 15-30 min. after and it feels great!

# Thread: Skin Temp's
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-temps.132/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### meyoolia@gmail.com — 2012-03-15T13:24:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-temps.132/post-659>

Hi all,

As I understood it, Dr. K. says the tub water should be between 50-55F.... but i seem to recall reading that we're aiming to get our \*skin\* to that temp, which mine never is, when I check it immediately after getting out after 30+ minutes. Think I should be lowering the tub temp? easily doable, with water coming straight outta Lake Ontario at about 40F  cheers J

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-15T15:40:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-temps.132/post-732>

My understanding is the same thing - get your skin to that temp. The water temp needs to be as low as necessary for your skin to reach that temp range. Of course always being on the lookout for frostbite/burns from the water or ice.

### kc_boxerman@yahoo.com — 2012-03-15T15:43:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-temps.132/post-735>

I think that is why it takes closer to an hour for the skin to reach the temp of the water?

### Claudia — 2012-03-15T20:29:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-temps.132/post-961>

What are you using to measure skin temp? I have an infrared thermometer that I use on the kids, it measures arterial temp. I tried it after a cold shower but I had to dry off first. I don't think it should get wet and I don't want to ruin it as I need it for the kids. After the cold shower my skin was 68 degrees. HOw accurate is this? I"m thinking not very accurate at all. My water temp is at 50-55 coming out of the tap. My cold bath last night was 12-13 minutes long. I will increase the next bath by 5 minutes. Do I need to spend more time in the water then to get my skin temp down to 50-55, same as the water? I am being very cautious and I can't use ice. I developed cold urticaria from doing a cold bath with ice last week. I am easing back in and trying not to let that be a deterrent.

### kc_boxerman@yahoo.com — 2012-03-15T21:20:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-temps.132/post-996>

Claudia - even at 45 minutes in about 52deg water my skin temp was still a bit above 55 degrees. But it was lower than 30 minutes I had been doing, so it seems to take a while. My measurements of water/skin were with my cooking thermapen. I pinched my skin around it to measure skin temp and it is a fast reading that should be pretty accurate. I use the thermapen a ton for cooking and even more now - worth the investment even if not the best tool for skin temps.

### AKMan — 2012-03-15T21:41:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-temps.132/post-1017>

I've put a lot of thought into this 55 deg skin temp thing. When I first started CT'ing last month, I could get my forearms down to 55 deg, no problem. If I went outside, where air temp was 0 deg, it would take about 10 minutes and my skin temp would measure 55 degs (I use an infrared thermometer). After a week or so, my skin temp would barely get to 70 deg with the same or longer exposure. I tried soaking in 35 deg water, it still wouldn't get down to 55 degrees. I think this is because with repeated cold exposure, your blood vessels don't constrict and allow more blood to flow to the surface of your skin.

What I think needs to happen for effective CT: You need to expose your skin to 55 degree water or air. In numerous studies I've read, researchers on CT use 50-55 degrees as the threshhold for shivering. When you start shivering, you are using a different mechanism to increase body heat (thermogenesis) that involves muscle contraction and ATP production. Non-shivering Thermogenesis (NST) uses uncoupling protein (UCP-1) to create heat from mitochodria outside the normal ATP cycle. This is the uniqueness of 55 degrees. It will happen at any temperature below thermoneutral, which is generally thought to be 72 degrees, but the closer you get to the threshold of shivering, the more you will benefit from CT.

55 degree water is 26 times more effective than 55 degree air. This is because water pulls heat away faster than air. In my N=1 experiments in Alaska, the air temp needs to be below 10 degrees to get the same effect as 55 degree water. In air temps between 0 and -20, most people will begin shivering in about 5-10 minutes (wearing only shorts).

Bottom line - CT will work in air or water temperatures between 50 and 70 degrees. It's effectiveness decreases when you start shivering. So, figure out where you start to shiver and get a tad warmer than that.

### Evo Mama — 2012-03-15T21:52:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-temps.132/post-1024>

If I went outside, where air temp was 0 deg, it would take about 10 minutes and my skin temp would measure 55 degs (I use an infrared thermometer). After a week or so, my skin temp would barely get to 70 deg with the same or longer exposure. I tried soaking in 35 deg water, it still wouldn't get down to 55 degrees. In numerous studies I've read, researchers on CT use 50-55 degrees as the threshhold for shivering. When you start shivering, you are using a different mechanism to increase body heat (thermogenesis) that involves muscle contraction and ATP production. Non-shivering Thermogenesis (NST) uses uncoupling protein (UCP-1) to create heat from mitochodria outside the normal ATP cycle. This is the uniqueness of 55 degrees. It will happen at any temperature below thermoneutral, which is generally thought to be 72 degrees, but the closer you get to the threshold of shivering, the more you will benefit from CT.
>
>
>
> 55 degree water is 26 times more effective than 55 degree air. This is because water pulls heat away faster than air. In my N=1 experiments in Alaska, the air temp needs to be below 10 degrees to get the same effect as 55 degree water. In air temps between 0 and -20, most people will begin shivering in about 5-10 minutes (wearing only shorts).
>
>
>
> Bottom line - CT will work in air or water temperatures between 50 and 70 degrees. It's effectiveness decreases when you start shivering. So, figure out where you start to shiver and get a tad warmer than that.

Does this mean if I'm shivering in the tube a good deal of the time, I need to increase the temperature of the water, so I'm just above that threshold, or will I still be making BAT if I'm shivering a lot. The thing is, with the water temps being consistent, one night I shiver and have a hard time, and the next night I shiver for a few minutes, then I'm fine. So, if my reaction (shivering) is inconsistent, how can I use shivering as a measure of whether or not the CT is being effective? What do you all think?

### AKMan — 2012-03-15T22:04:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-temps.132/post-1034>

I'd love to hear Jack's opinion, but I think CT should be done above the shivering level. When you shiver, it is your muscles that are warming you up, not the uncoupled proteins from BAT. To me, shivering during CT therapy would be the same as wrapping yourself in hot towels while in a tub of ice.

### Kpcst — 2012-03-16T03:47:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-temps.132/post-1248>

I'm shivering right now as I read this forum on the iPad in my cold dark hotel room in order to distract myself from the cold tub. Yes, that was a run on sentence.

### Kiera2 — 2012-03-16T05:11:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-temps.132/post-1281>

> **AKMan;1035 wrote:** I'd love to hear Jack's opinion, but I think CT should be done above the shivering level. When you shiver, it is your muscles that are warming you up, not the uncoupled proteins from BAT. To me, shivering during CT therapy would be the same as wrapping yourself in hot towels while in a tub of ice.

On Jack's CT-6 blog, comment #634,TheKid asked if it was ok that he was still shivering an hour after CT.

Jack's response: @The Kid as much as your body can dish outÃ¢â‚¬Â¦..that is a great sign your pilot is turning on.

So, if shivering is a good sign of progress, I don't see how avoiding it can be a positive?

I shivered when I started CT; the first couple of times I shivered in the bath for a while, but then I shivered after getting out for a few sessions.

I don't shiver at all in the bath now, and afterward, if it's a cold day like today, I have this kind of low hummm in my body. But not real shivering.

If we were supposed to avoid temperatures that cause shivering, I'm sure that would have been mentioned in the protocol.

### Claudia — 2012-03-16T05:20:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-temps.132/post-1286>

I don't shiver at all when I'm in the bath. Tonight I did 16 minutes in 53 degree water. Not enough water to be fully submerged so my stomach didn't get fully wet. I did shiver when I got out and put some clothes on as quickly as I could and socks and felt better once I started moving a bit. I'm still cold now 3 hours later but manageable cold. Is this ok?

# Thread: Hi ya'll! Martin from north Georgia, USA
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-yall-martin-from-north-georgia-usa.134/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Martin — 2012-03-15T13:48:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-yall-martin-from-north-georgia-usa.134/post-668>

So good to see that so many people from all over are gathering for what I think is a MAJOR turning point in our lives. I finally see a path out of my imbalances and have already seen and felt a change for the better.

I'm a 5'11" 145 lb. 59 year-old male, retired from the greatest job ever - restoring old Craftsman Bungalow homes in historic neighborhoods in Atlanta. I still miss it, but retiring at age 48 was a miracle that my wife and I pulled off with lots of hard work and frugal living.

We live on 35 acres up in the beautiful north Georgia mountains where I have a large garden (organic, of course), a small orchard, two workshops, big garden shed, and a diesel tractor. Every lad's dream, no?!

Struggled in this life with health issues for as long as I can remember. Mom passed down hypo-T, adrenal stuff, and an interest in improving myself.

I recently found out that I have mold susceptibility haplotype and low alpha MSH haplotype through testing my DNA. I have appointment with great mold doc and have introduced Dr. Kruse's work to him and his biotoxin study group.

I'm on my way out of imbalance.

Martin

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/669=4-IMG_4626.jpg)
