### persistence — 2014-03-10T22:18:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114603>

> [Optimalbound said:](/goto/post?id=114514)
>
> Jack has several times called bullshit on Jaminet's musings about "safe starches".
> [http://jackkruse.com/the-rewarding-feeling-of-safe-starches/](http://jackkruse.com/the-rewarding-feeling-of-safe-starches/)

If one diet says "eat no more than 50 grams of starchy carbs" and the other diet says "eat 50 to 120 grams of starchy carbs" these are not radically different diets. They are different degrees of a low-carb diet.

50 grams of carb is a few small slices of yam. It's almost nothing. I think too many people in the Paleo community spend too much time debating differences between diets that are more similar than not.

### persistence — 2014-03-10T22:45:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114605>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=114516)
>
> I heard Jimmy Moore say to Professor Cordain once that Paul Jaminet had said that his (Cordain's) diet had given him scurvy and that he'd said that starches this and starches that ...
>
> Professor Cordain basically said, "I don't know of him" (as much as to say, he may move in your world but not in mine) and "My diet can't give you scurvy." A little _de haut en bas_ but fair comment given the provocation I thought. As for starches, Cordain went though the stuff on glycemic load for Mr. Moore.
>
> I don't think it's true to say that "Paleo is really a ... ketogenic diet", Pone. If you look at Cordain's books, and journal papers, he actually recommends quite a lot of carbohydrate. He's not offering just a twist on what, say, Phinney, Volek and Westman are offering. He also reckons that the macronutrient ratios he recommends reflect what current groups of hunter-gatherers actually do. Since Cordain doesn't recommend cereal grains or legumes, principally because of the antinutrients in them, (and doesn't recommend sugar/HFCS either), and fights shy of potatoes because of their glycemic index, he achieves those macronutrient ratios on fruit.
>
> There's nothing, absolutely nothing, in _The Paleo Diet_ or _The Paleo Answer_ on ketosis. The topic isn't even mentioned.
>
> Jack, of course, would not have you eat fruit out of season. Standard Paleo has never talked much about seasonal eating.

I think Jack's description of Paleo as "bro science" is perfect. The fact is no one can define Paleo exactly. For some it is a high carb diet that uses lots of tubers, rice, fruits. For some it is a low carb diet that stresses under 120 grams of carbs. If you visit forums like Mark's Daily Apple, it is a free for all where Paleo means anything the speaker wants it to mean. When some term can be defined so loosely, it almost stops meaning anything.

It is possible to read some Paleo diet books and end up eating only vegetables that are non-starchy. Because there are no explicit carb targets, the adherent of such a diet does not realize that they are effectively on a ketogenic diet. That's exactly what happened to me. I went on a Paleo compliant diet with zero starchy carbs, lots of nutritious vegetables, animal meats, and lots of healthy fats. It was effectively a ketogenic diet, although no one ever called it that, and I never thought going in that this is what it was.

Whether it was ketogenic or not, I crashed on that diet. I lost a lot of weight that I could not afford to lose, and I have had a hard time getting it back because of other problems I have. I will post a separatel thread on my issues in meet and greet later.

Jaminet isn't perfect either, but his insight that vegetables alone give almost zero net carb utilization over the energy required to digest them really simplifies thinking about carbs. Assuming you have a target of 50 carbs a day, getting those from starchy sources at least guarantees that those 50 grams are above and beyond the amounts needed to digest the starch. And if you are eating 50 to 100 grams of starchy carbs a day, this is a low carb diet. 50 grams of starch a day is nothing. It's four small slices of yam.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-11T08:35:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114749>

> [Pone said:](/goto/post?id=114605)
>
> I think Jack's description of Paleo as "bro science" is perfect.

Bro? Short for broken?

> The fact is no one can define Paleo exactly.

Well, it's just a name; it doesn't really require a "definition". I think Cordain came up with the name as a punchy name for his diet. He was latching on to stuff coming out from Boyd Eaton and collaborators, although Boyd Eaton didn't use that name:

[http://www.amazon.com/The-Paleolithic-Prescription-Program-Exercise/dp/0060158719/](http://www.amazon.com/The-Paleolithic-Prescription-Program-Exercise/dp/0060158719/)

I guess Cordain thought: lop the "lithic" off, and it'll make a good short marketing name.

> If you visit forums like Mark's Daily Apple, it is a free for all where Paleo means anything the speaker wants it to mean.

Posters on a forum post what they choose, don't they? Mark Sisson himself doesn't actually use the term. He refers to himself as "primal". _His_ marketing-term, if you like. Mark's version is more like a traditional low-carb diet -- but then most people in the past who ever wrote about prehistoric diets before Boyd Eaton and Cordain came along seem to have considered that before the advent of farming diets were fairly low-carb.

Nothing to stress over anyway. It's just a name. Essentially, to Cordain it seems to mean most of all: I don't advise cereal grains or dairy products, because these are evolutionarily novel foods.

> … I crashed on ... diet \[X\]. I lost a lot of weight that I could not afford to lose, and I have had a hard time getting it back because of other problems I have. I will post a separatel thread on my issues in meet and greet later.

Then why were you asking about going on the Leptin Reset ℞ ?

I may be wrong, but my understanding is that ℞ is for people who are severely _over_\-weight. That's what I understood Jack's blogs on it to say; that's what I understood the book to say; that's what Jack seems to say in podcast interviews.

>

> Posters on a forum post what they choose, don't they? Mark Sisson himself doesn't actually use the term. He refers to himself as "primal". _His_ marketing-term, if you like. Mark's version is more like a traditional low-carb diet -- but then most people in the past who ever wrote about prehistoric diets before Boyd Eaton and Cordain came along seem to have considered that before the advent of farming diets were fairly low-carb.
>
> Nothing to stress over anyway. It's just a name. Essentially, to Cordain it seems to mean most of all: I don't advise cereal grains or dairy products, because these are evolutionarily novel foods.
>
>
>
> Then why were you asking about going on the Leptin Reset ℞ ?
>
> I may be wrong, but my understanding is that ℞ is for people who are severely _over_\-weight. That's what I understood Jack's blogs on it to say; that's what I understood the book to say; that's what Jack seems to say in podcast interviews.

I was thinking "Bro" more like two guys talking to each other:

B1: "Hey Bro, I'm on Paleo."
B2: "Cool Bro, I'm on Paleo too."

Brother #1 eats 60% carbohydrates from starchy carbs.
Brother #2 eats a ketogenic diet made of non starchy vegetables.

These diets have very little in common, yet the people in the conversation believe they are the same diet. That's the essence of what Paleo is - to my understanding - in popular forums. People who don't measure or understand macronutrient profiles talk about radically different diets and call them all Paleo because the poster has a caveman in his profile photo. That's what those diets have in common.

Mark Sisson is great. People who post on his forums are confused.

I am skinny not fat. I \*probably\* do not have Leptin resistance. How would I measure that?

I probably \*do\* have insulin resistance, which largely explains why I also have terrible LDL particle counts. Constant high insulin produces too many lipids.

Diabetes affects different people in different ways. The most difficult cases are overweight people because the fat itself becomes implicated in the disease. My case is typical of many over 35: metabolism just gets inefficient as you age. You get insulin resistance. Most people don't understand the implication and that in turn becomes heart disease, cancer, whatever.

I'm trying to correct the condition before it does permanent damage. I'm not more than 20% of the way there yet.

### caroline — 2014-03-11T09:49:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114762>

Obese folks and anorexia are opposite ends of the same spectrum ......

Have you had a Saliva ASI done? with diurnal cortisol? What about a complete gut panel - I think it is by Genova.

Dr. jackson says that 98% of people have gut issues and that is where he starts - doesn't even care about testing at first....but hormones play a huge role too - you can't fix one without working on the other

### persistence — 2014-03-12T00:12:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114867>

Gagnrad, I have nothing but respect for Cordain. He is nuanced and most of his arguments are clear in scope and structure. I don't have to agree with everything he says to respect him.

The more I study nutrition, the more I realize that 70%+ of good health as you age comes down to glucose, insulin, glucagon, and leptin. Manage these things well and you avoid most of the chronic diseases of aging. You will live as long as your genetics allow, except for a freak statistical outlier event. What makes this hard to do - even if you are motivated - is that we only have a way to measure glucose at home. To get the other three you have to find labs and it doesn't give you much immediate feedback day to day.

At this point - to my thinking - a diet to be worth studying and understanding has to tell a competent story about how it manages those four things. Love them or hate them, Dr Rosedale, Paul Jaminet, and Jack Kruse all have diets that closely tie to these concepts and tell a competent story about how to control these elements. You can differ with them on many details, but in the big picture they are low carbohydrate diets. They are serious people with serious ideas, and we all learn from them. Macronutrient ratios are a critical theme in all of these diets. For Rosedale, he wants you to be ketogenic. For Kruse, apparently he wants you no more than 50 gm of starchy carbs per day, preferably less. For Jaminet, 50 grams to 120 grams of carbs, with a typical target being about 20% of total caloric intake in carbs. If you want to hedge bets, you could be at 50 gm of carbs and probably all three of them would say you are at least close to compliance.

I respect Mark Sisson a lot because he is also nuanced and is not dogmatic. But the people on his forums are largely just trend followers who can't define their diet with any consistency. Some of the people on his forums are eating extremely high carbohydrate diets. They have no frigging clue whether they are prediabetic, and if they were they wouldn't even care. I have had people on those forums tell me "Why do you worry so much about blood sugar? You need to just get outside and smell fresh air." So Paleo diet for such a person means trying to enjoy life, and eat natural foods, but high in carbohydrates, and if there are a lot of fruits, that is also high in fructose and sugars. People in such forums also sometimes make recipes with agave sugar because it is "natural", so let's add some pure liquid sucrose into the fire.

I apologize for being difficult. But I find it difficult to talk to such people. They are killing themselves with uncontrolled carbohydrates and don't want to invest a single neuron in understanding anything. I am very flawed. I have very limited understanding of things. At least I am trying to solve the right problems, or am open to understanding what the right problems are so that I can solve them.

I do not restrict food. I eat a lot of calories and would be happy to eat more if I could.

I do not have an irrational fear of weight gain. I want to gain weight not lose it.

If you think Wikipedia has a wrong definition of anorexia, I am not the person to argue that. Their definition seems to have a lot in common with every other publication I have seen. I know almost nothing about anorexia, I'm sorry.

By any reasonable definition, I am not anorexic.

### sooperb — 2014-03-12T11:49:32+0200

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-12T13:13:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114987>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=114981)
>
> Like your hand and your arm? Your foot and your leg? Connected but separate?

It might be closer than that. It might be simply that mind and body are the _same_ thing seen from two different aspects.

There are all kinds of experiences that suggest that. There are also some arguments that do.

Otherwise, there's a dualist view: that the mind is in the body like a sailor in a ship. That's Descartes' formulation, but the notion is much older, and comes into Western thought through Plato.

But you end up with problems, such as: if the mind is something fundamentally different from the body how does the one act upon the other? People tried to fudge that by positing some intermediate substance, failing to see that that doesn't solve the problem. How does the intermediate substance act on both ends of the bridge it forms? Back where we started.

Medieval Christian beliefs are sometimes expressed in terms of a body/soul dualism, but it's really coming out of Plato. AFAIK, the Jews didn't really think in that way.

After Descartes the dualist view was fairly firmly imbedded in philosophy, but began to be challenged in different ways later on. William James does it one way, Wittgenstein in another.

But I'm not a philosopher, and I don't take a view on it. I'd sooner say I don't know. I don't think there's any firm grounds to believe in dualism, though.

It's a different issue, but I am sceptical of "the talking cure" in just about anything. I think it may help as part a wider approach. Its results don't seem to be impressive. Even assuming that, say, a Freudian understanding of a particular problem someone might have was correct (dubious, IMO), does understanding necessarily bring resolution? I think psychotherapists have made some very large claims but have let a lot of people down while making pretty large inroads into their wallets.

I have heard that anorexia is another condition for which there have been reports that neurofeedback is helpful. If that can be substabtiated, I think that would be interesting. Apparently, the University of Graz is currently looking into it:

[http://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT01390493](http://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT01390493)

Four days into CT (n=1) and got a head/sinus cold. (I am still cycling chills and fever.) I stopped the CT (n=1) experiment till I feel better. But should I continue my mild introduction into CT anyway.?I use mild (wimpy)Theraflex gel ice packs , not real ice or the tub yet. I'm open to all experimentation.(as long as its wimpy/slow/steady) EDIT:4/8/12: Three weeks progress report. This is much easier then I would have originally guessed. I alternate cold baths and Gel packs daily. Water 58 degrees for an hour and 14 square feet of theraflex packs at a time. The Packs are actually colder. I live in Florida, so I've had to ramp up my water cool down "technology". Had to go from 40 lbs.of ice(gallon plastic jugs) to eighty lbs for tomorow.Will eighty lbs. fit into the tub? Will there be room for me? We'll see. The gel packs may prove easier for Florida.I can load up over twenty square feet of the stuff. Sort of like being buried in very cold sand. But my original question still stands. Do you ct while sick?

I have been supplementing D3 for over 4 years and have not been sick one day during that time. Used to get 3 a year. And CT also strengthens the immune system.

Do not be discouraged. You are in the right place for optimal health.

# Thread: No Gallbladder

### Marsha — 2012-03-15T22:41:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-gallbladder.176/post-1059>

I wish there was a section for those without their gallbladder.

I know all the info is inside of Dr.K's blog posts and their comments that follow, but there is so much for a beginer to go through and still not be caught up! I'm so confused over what supplements to take and which parts of what protocol to follow. I've gain so many inches since starting the Reset that it's kind of scary. My labs are not back yet, so haven't been too vocal of all of this, but wanted to at least put the idea out there to the Forum Gurus.

### jeepifer@gmail.com — 2012-03-15T22:52:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-gallbladder.176/post-1061>

I'm interested in hearing the replies as well... I haven't had any gains, though. I just need to watch the fat intake and make sure to take my digestive enzymes.

### TerrierMom — 2012-03-22T20:29:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-gallbladder.176/post-4585>

Along with the NO GALLBLADDER section it would be nice to have one for people with Gastric Bypass. My sister had this and guess what, didn't lose all she wanted to lose. She'd like to try this but has neither a gallbladder or part of her intestine (I guess that is what they cut out...) - so where to start?

### japhyryder — 2013-10-22T20:35:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-gallbladder.176/post-98585>

Yes, I'm adding another voice to this. I have scoured the web, and there is no where with any in-depth, genuinely insightful advice for those with no gallbladder. Ox bile, betaine, digestive enzymes, lecithin have all helped me, but the chronic depression and OCD remains.

### nicld — 2013-10-22T21:38:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-gallbladder.176/post-98599>

Just my 2 cents but I was never told ANYTHING about what foods to avoid after having mine out 16 years ago. They even tried to feed me spaghetti when I was in after the surgery. I think after a while to body just compensates since I don't think that I am having any issues from eating a high fat diet but not sure. Very curious to hear what other people have to say too.

### cantweight — 2013-10-22T22:38:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-gallbladder.176/post-98603>

I have no gallbladder and have healed my gut quite well.

Digestion GB from pure encapsulations is a supplement I took while doing the leptin rx and it helped with tolerating the fat. That was 18 months ago, I no longer use any type of digestive enzyme and do well with a paleo/epi paleo diet. Even high fat I am digesting fine where as prior to the leptin rx it all went out in bowels...sorry if that is TMI The only thing that causes me any trouble is fruit.

That said i am still struggling with weight loss but that is more of a sleep/light/EMF issue for me.

### caroline — 2013-10-22T23:30:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-gallbladder.176/post-98610>

I had my gallbladder out when I was quite young and have had no problems with all the fat - in fact - I prefer fat to anything else. Maybe I am one of the lucky ones.

Just hang in there Marsha until you get your labs back and the post them in the lab section for help with them.

Please start a journal in the journal section. Dr k. Talked about this in the Q&A again yesterday. He can't really help you at all and offer suggestions unless he knows something about you ... He needs some context.

Please tell us where you live and if you have children etc etc - these are all a big part of the puzzle.

Do you have Jack's book? It is a great overview of all of this. Have you listened to JK's podcasts? They have a ton of information - so an easy way to get information fast.

The webinars and follow up Q&A are an amazing resource that you shouldn't miss if at all possible.

Please keep reading and asking questions....

Have you read the easy start guides? C/T is huge to reduce inflammation....

### Jack Kruse — 2013-10-23T02:19:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-gallbladder.176/post-98664>

There is a blog post on missing a gallbladder.............

### japhyryder — 2013-10-23T11:40:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-gallbladder.176/post-98711>

Yes, I read that post Jack, thanks. Superb. However, since I know you're not keen to suggest supplementation too overtly over the web, it leaves us (the gallbladder-less) not entirely sure how to proceed. In my case, this experience had a profound effect on my mental health. I'm following Epi Paleo Rx as of recently, using Ox bile, betaine and lipase to try and process the fats. Moderate improvements, but my mind is not what it was..... Am going to try supplementing NAC, MSM, and DMSO as you've recommended on the post to see how that helps but i'm reluctant to become a supplement junkie if I can do it with diet....

### Jude — 2013-10-23T11:53:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-gallbladder.176/post-98712>

That blog came out in 2011 iirc, and it really helped me. !0yrs post cholecystectomy and I could tolerate no fats...lived on fruit and carbs till I found Dr K. By then I had very high Trigs.....

The instigation of Ox bile into my JK recommended Leptin reset made all my probs go away. I have found it necessary to continue with it daily, otherwise I have lots of indigestion pains 2+ hrs later. Have also experimented with Betaine Hcl each breakfast, and if I have been diligent with it, somehow ...can manage a couple of fatty meals without the help of ox bile if I have forgotten to have with me when out..which is nothing short of amazing for me.

Don't forget somewhere JK recommends that the gallbladderless also need Vit K2 cause we aren't able to recycle our own because of the missing GB. Never previously been warned about that either...or the fact that ox bile just might be necessary for us!

And then that leads to a very important read for the gallbladderless.....the 3 blogs on osteoporosis..which is where our bones head(go) if we don't do the Vit K2. Hope this helps somewhat.

### japhyryder — 2013-10-23T13:06:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-gallbladder.176/post-98714>

This is excellent Jude. Leaves me taking a lot of supplements though! Thanks for your help....

### Liveea — 2013-10-24T18:43:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-gallbladder.176/post-98878>

I don't have a gallbladder either and never got any update on how to eat or anything. I had no stones. It just stopped functioning (2% function) and they figured it created its own blockage. My surgeon passed suddenly shortly after my procedure. I continued for a year to have problems with gallbladder symptoms and pain.

I am new here. So, I am just beginning this journey. Looking forward to it. I have a lot of fear but I am going to do it (fear of being cold and I am in North Dakota, lol).

I should probably go back and post some information about myself and some health history. Thank you for everything!!

# Thread: Melatonin = More Effective CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-more-effective-ct.179/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-03-15T23:33:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-more-effective-ct.179/post-1076>

From a recent study, notice any other themes from Dr. K's blogs? : [http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-789X.2010.00756.x/abstract;jsessionid=2CBACA76EADCB416B16C710DD93437A7.d02t01?systemMessage=Wiley+Online+Library+will+be+disrupted+17+March+from+10-14+GMT+%2806-10+EDT%29+for+essential+maintenance&userIsAuthenticated=false&deniedAccessCustomisedMessage=](http://http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-789X.2010.00756.x/abstract;jsessionid=2CBACA76EADCB416B16C710DD93437A7.d02t01?systemMessage=Wiley+Online+Library+will+be+disrupted+17+March+from+10-14+GMT+%2806-10+EDT%29+for+essential+maintenance&userIsAuthenticated=false&deniedAccessCustomisedMessage=)

A worldwide increase in the incidence of obesity indicates the unsuccessful battle against this disorder. Obesity and the associated health problems urgently require effective strategies of treatment. The new discovery that a substantial amount of functional brown adipose tissue (BAT) is retained in adult humans provides a potential target for treatment of human obesity. BAT is active metabolically and disposes of extra energy via generation of heat through uncoupling oxidative phosphorylation in mitochondria. The physiology of BAT is readily regulated by melatonin, which not only increases recruitment of brown adipocytes but also elevates their metabolic activity in mammals. It is speculated that the hypertrophic effect and functional activation of BAT induced by melatonin may likely apply to the human. Thus, melatonin, a naturally occurring substance with no reported toxicity, may serve as a novel approach for treatment of obesity. Conversely, because of the availability of artificial light sources, excessive light exposure after darkness onset in modern societies should be considered a potential contributory factor to human obesity as light at night dramatically reduces endogenous melatonin production. In the current article, the potential associations of melatonin, BAT, obesity and the medical implications are discussed.

### Lyndra — 2012-03-16T00:58:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-more-effective-ct.179/post-1119>

Good stuff. So much is coming together. Gotta go get some blueblocker glasses...

### Croak — 2012-03-16T02:05:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-more-effective-ct.179/post-1168>

I think the Doc is going to come back and say you don't need the melatonin supplementation, that the CT and circadian light management will induce the same effects.

### AKMan — 2012-03-16T02:18:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-more-effective-ct.179/post-1177>

> **Croak;1169 wrote:** I think the Doc is going to come back and say you don't need the melatonin supplementation, that the CT and circadian light management will induce the same effects.

Light management is key! That was my point.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-16T06:03:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-more-effective-ct.179/post-1297>

> **AKMan;1178 wrote:** Light management is key! That was my point.

Definitely - he said in that podcast that he thinks melatonin supplements are only for jet lag and nothing else.

### x_danny_x — 2012-03-18T05:27:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-more-effective-ct.179/post-2303>

I still take melotonin. I like it when it helps release growth hormones. Good for a workout with heavy weights.

### Kasra — 2012-03-27T20:04:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-more-effective-ct.179/post-7214>

> **Croak;1169 wrote:** I think the Doc is going to come back and say you don't need the melatonin supplementation, that the CT and circadian light management will induce the same effects.

I agree that cold adaptation and light management are two profoundly useful tools in regulating circadian rhythm and optimizing metabolism. The study linked by AKMan corroborates this.

However, several studies linked in that same source show that endogenous melatonin production _drastically_ decreases with age. I imagine that being cold adapted and wearing blue-blocker glasses could could only mitigate this.

Thus, I think you should not be so quick to discard the idea of melatonin supplementation, especially in light of its exceptional (albeit largely anecdotal) safety record.

### Croak — 2012-03-27T21:58:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-more-effective-ct.179/post-7313>

And I'm pretty sure the response is still going to be no need to supplement, period. Kruse has clearly stated he's dead set against melatonin supplements. As a tool to reset the circadian clocks after jet lag, he's ok with it, but that's a day's use here and there, not long-term supplementation.

Remember, his specialty isn't weight loss or fitness, it's _longevity_. Taking into that account, I'm guessing he's seeing the pineal gland working just fine in older patients following his protocols, and I suspect that supplements can exacerbate the loss of function.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-03-28T15:43:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-more-effective-ct.179/post-7688>

What about SAD light therapy boxes? Would those still be okay to use?

Typically, you expose yourself to the light boxes for 20-40 minutes in the morning.

### kalibex — 2012-03-28T16:13:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-more-effective-ct.179/post-7697>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;7728 wrote:** What about SAD light therapy boxes? Would those still be okay to use?
>
> Typically, you expose yourself to the light boxes for 20-40 minutes in the morning.

You know what would be interesting? If CT turned out to negate the need for such SAD treatments... What if it turns out that we (if properly cold-adapted) would feel less depressed in the cold season, but would naturally show slightly altered behavior patterns in the winter (which we would have to understand, and adapt to).... If so, that would be another nice CT benefit...

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-28T17:00:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-more-effective-ct.179/post-7721>

> **Croak;7351 wrote:** And I'm pretty sure the response is still going to be no need to supplement, period. Kruse has clearly stated he's dead set against melatonin supplements. As a tool to reset the circadian clocks after jet lag, he's ok with it, but that's a day's use here and there, not long-term supplementation.

This confuses me because isn't melatonin one of his top 10 supplements? He recommends it along with DHEA for at least the first 6 months?

### JC2K — 2012-03-16T00:14:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/puffiness-in-face.181/post-1104>

Why are you still doing face dunks if you're doing cold showers and ice packs? The protocol goes from face dunks to ice packs to shower/baths. Also, a week? A bit fast isn't it? Or are you already acclimatized to the cold?

### ste colombe — 2012-03-25T12:57:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/puffiness-in-face.181/post-5744>

> **codys182;1088 wrote:** I am about a week into CT and I think I am losing body fat but my face is getting very puffy. Anyone else experienced this? I am doing cold showers, ice packs, and face dunks.

I have been doing ice baths for 2 weeks now (no longer doing face dunking)

and also get puffy under my eyes. I am also getting really bad headaches behind my eyes and top of my head. Does anyone else get awful headaches?

### meyoolia@gmail.com — 2012-04-04T05:00:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/puffiness-in-face.181/post-11125>

> **ste colombe;5778 wrote:** I have been doing ice baths for 2 weeks now (no longer doing face dunking)
>
> and also get puffy under my eyes. I am also getting really bad headaches behind my eyes and top of my head. Does anyone else get awful headaches?

BE CAREFUL!!!! don't be afraid to go slow. I've had to quit ALL CT because of what I've only realized now seems to be a VERY nasty head full of cold urticaria from either going too quick or too cold. I'd gotten as far a a few tubbings without a problem until i dropped the tub temp just a little too far or too long and whump: over 2 weeks of spreading, painful, swollen, crazy itchy rash behind ears, back of head and down back of neck. My naturopath suggested neem oil yesterday and that's FINALLY beginning to give a bit of relief. Was it just the CT? the combination of CT and BHRT (which I've also stopped)? god only knows, but I'm wiser and a little fearful! I'd love to see some cautions and suggestions from Dr. Kruse in addition to the group's "gung ho, we're in this together"

# Thread: Another meal timing question
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-meal-timing-question.183/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### blindpig — 2012-03-16T01:10:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-meal-timing-question.183/post-1128>

I read the thread down below about meal timing but I'm still not clear if what I have been doing is the best. I have been doing the reset since November, but I know I am still not LS on all levels, so I continue.

For quite some time, this has been the pattern of my appetite: I get up at 5:00am and eat within 30-45 minutes. I have plenty of protein and plenty of fat (I would be embarassed to say how much coconut oil and butter I put in my BP coffee every morning). Then I am not hungry for about 7 to 8 hours, but I am hungry at that point and it would be difficult to wait any longer. I eat a large lunch then, with lots of protein and fat again. After that I am not hungry again in time for dinner, so I normally don't have any dinner. I do sometimes get hungry before bed, but then it's too late, so I just don't eat. If I do eat anything before my "3 hours before bed window" closes, it will usually just be a cup of bone broth with a shot of hot sauce or something similar.

This has been serving me well, I believe, in fact I have experimented and when I have eaten a larger evening meal I have woken up hungry in the night. But my concern is that by not eating from roughly 1:00pm to 5:00am this is too much like IFing, which I know I should not be doing at this point. Opinions, anyone?

### Groketteliveson — 2012-03-16T01:19:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-meal-timing-question.183/post-1131>

> **blindpig;1129 wrote:** I read the thread down below about meal timing but I'm still not clear if what I have been doing is the best. I have been doing the reset since November, but I know I am still not LS on all levels, so I continue.
>
>
>
> For quite some time, this has been the pattern of my appetite: I get up at 5:00am and eat within 30-45 minutes. I have plenty of protein and plenty of fat (I would be embarassed to say how much coconut oil and butter I put in my BP coffee every morning). Then I am not hungry for about 7 to 8 hours, but I am hungry at that point and it would be difficult to wait any longer. I eat a large lunch then, with lots of protein and fat again. After that I am not hungry again in time for dinner, so I normally don't have any dinner. I do sometimes get hungry before bed, but then it's too late, so I just don't eat. If I do eat anything before my "3 hours before bed window" closes, it will usually just be a cup of bone broth with a shot of hot sauce or something similar.
>
>
>
> This has been serving me well, I believe, in fact I have experimented and when I have eaten a larger evening meal I have woken up hungry in the night. But my concern is that by not eating from roughly 1:00pm to 5:00am this is too much like IFing, which I know I should not be doing at this point. Opinions, anyone?

You could probably classify that as IFing but I would go by how you feel and if you don't feel hungry then don't eat. I don't see anything wrong with that.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-03-16T02:56:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-meal-timing-question.183/post-1207>

Maybe try a smaller lunch earlier in the day, so you can have dinner at the appropriate time.

### blindpig — 2012-03-16T18:35:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-meal-timing-question.183/post-1569>

> **Groketteliveson;1132 wrote: You could probably classify that as IFing but I would go by how you feel and if you don't feel hungry then don't eat. I don't see anything wrong with that.
>
>
> PaleoCowgirl;1208 wrote:** Maybe try a smaller lunch earlier in the day, so you can have dinner at the appropriate time.

Wahhhhh! I agree with both of you, so I still don't know which is optimal!

### Birdy — 2012-03-16T18:47:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-meal-timing-question.183/post-1581>

It IS like IF'ing, but the important thing is to not IF at breakfast, so if you feel fine with it, no problem.

### blindpig — 2012-03-16T18:53:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-meal-timing-question.183/post-1584>

> **Birdy;1583 wrote:** It IS like IF'ing, but the important thing is to not IF at breakfast, so if you feel fine with it, no problem.

Thanks for breaking the tie, Birdy (that used to be a nickname of mine!)... I will quit worrying about the "rules" and stay with what's working for me. And goodness knows this is working for me, it is nothing short of amazing.

### Lyndra — 2012-03-16T01:24:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ladies-in-the-cold-tub.184/post-1134>

I don't need to lose any fat from my breasts so I am making sure to keep my chest out of the tub. That makes it a little harder to fully cover the tummy. Are you ladies taking any precautions to protect the girls?

### vkiernan — 2012-03-16T01:30:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ladies-in-the-cold-tub.184/post-1141>

> **Lyndra;1135 wrote:** I don't need to lose any fat from my breasts so I am making sure to keep my chest out of the tub. That makes it a little harder to fully cover the tummy. Are you ladies taking any precautions to protect the girls?

Nope cuz Dr. K said estrogen is there too and needs to be detoxed. So, even though I don't have much I am pouring water over my throat and chest while in 50 degree water. I want to be healthy at some point in my life and if that means the little things get littler, than so be it. If I am cold before I get in the tub and no I can't get the upper half that day, I keep pouring the water over my belly too. It turns pink so I'm pretty sure I'm good with getting it cold. I have thought about bringing ice packs in for it but I also realize this should be a limited thing cuz I really need to get more of me in the water, not less. Good luck to you.

### Glamorama — 2012-03-16T02:45:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ladies-in-the-cold-tub.184/post-1194>

The good thing is that it means the crappy WAT is going!

### Souldanzer — 2012-03-16T03:03:55+0200

Embrace it. There's certain places you gain fat when you're estrogen dominant - chest being one of them. So what you had wasn't healthy. I'd say it's one of the sure signs you're getting closer to optimal.

I'm in the camp of the smaller the better/more practical  I pretty much never wear a bra anymore and once you've ditched it for a while, you won't be able to stand wearing one anymore! So unnatural to have a tight rubber band around your chest... Plus there is a high correlation between breast cancer and bras... the more you wear a bra the more likely you are to get breast cancer (that's why I ditched them).

Note: It's a correlation not causal relationship between bra wearing and breast cancer. NEVER EVER wear a bra at night and try to go without one as much as possible. PSA over

### IceBarbie — 2012-03-16T03:30:25+0200

DIM is diindolylmethane. It helps reduce excessive Estrogen in the body for men & women. I have my hubby taking it now too his man-boobs (MOOBS). I get my DIM at either Amazon.com or iherb.com. I've been using the Olympian Labs brand. 400 mg's daily is the usual dose I believe. I learned about it from VKierman on here a few months ago. Milk Thistle is good for the liver and for reducing excessive Reverse T3's. Not sure right now if it helps with Estrogen or not...sorry to confuse you my listing it in my estrogen rant...lol!

### Adria — 2012-03-18T00:07:16+0200

I'm week 4 CT and have been paleo-keto for 3 years. Since paleo-keto the girls have gotton bigger, fatter. For CT I take my baths up to my waist. My bottom and thighs are already much thinner. I'm fitting in my smallest pants, had to dig them out of my closet. I'm not taking ice baths, just cold about 65 degrees. After my bath I dry up and then use ice packs, I sit on 2 - one for each cheek, and the others I move around on my shoulders, stomach, chest, neck, head. So far the girls seem the same size but if its better, healthier to dunk, then I'll start icing them even tho I"m not very well-endowed!!

### meyoolia@gmail.com — 2012-03-18T15:25:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ladies-in-the-cold-tub.184/post-2361>

though I don't need it yet - Lake Ontario water in the pipes is still PLENTY cold!! - I've been spot placing the home-made icepacks in the tub. EASY to make (thank you's to whoever it was on the blog!) and in the long run, likely cheaper than bags and bags and bags of ice.

1 part rubbing alcohol (isopropyl alcohol) to 2 parts water in a ziplock bag, double bagged to help minimize leakage. Made my 'ice vest' with those and duct tape, worked a treat  now that I don't need it anymore, I'll be recycling the contents into more ziplock baggies to spot place and use in the tub if the Lake warms up too much.

### Toni1675 — 2012-03-19T18:46:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ladies-in-the-cold-tub.184/post-2800>

\[When I do a CT Bath - my tub covers up to my waist... but that's mostly b/c we have an old post war 1950s house = old small bath tub that I Barely fit in and I'm 5'3"!

I keep wanting to go out to spa world - and hit their cold plunge pool.... I may just do it one day when I can swing the time! \]

Hi

I'm a 6'1" female and totally understand your problem so my remedy was to go out and buy a 100gal horse troff and put it on my patio. It actually works out nice I have cold water up to my neck now with the rest of my body submerged as well. The water also stays cold so I reuse it and save money on my water bill...LOL.

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-19T19:12:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ladies-in-the-cold-tub.184/post-2816>

>
>
>

I'm worried about my water bill after 3 weeks of filling a tub nearly 4x week too, but I'm afraid leaves and debri will get in anything I have outdoors. More thoughts on this...Thanks for sharing your idea.

# Thread: Liver - do you or don't you
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 58
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lyndra — 2012-03-16T01:41:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1152>

I haven't been able to bring myself to make liver, after a nasty experience years ago when I stunk up the house for days. I know it has all sorts of health benefits so I'd like to get to the point where I can tolerate it.

Is there any "mild" form of liver? Better ways to prepare it so it won't smell so much? I bought some liverwurst at the deli one day, and it didn't taste bad, but I worry about what else is in it.

### Gemini — 2012-03-16T02:16:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1174>

This may sound a bit odd - - - but here it goes,

I grill my liver - I use a marinade (term is used very lightly) of left over bacon grease, some salt and pepper, just smear grease over liver slices then salt and pepper to taste.

Put it on the grill using indirect heat - it does not take too long to cook, and it is delicious!!!

If I happen to take it off the grill too soon, I just finish the cooking in a skillet, but the grill flavor remains.

Beef liver is the strongest in my opinion - maybe try lamb or veal liver.

I will use chicken livers (usually from whole foods market) and make a chinese dish - I make my own soy sauce using water, lime juice, and some blackstrap molasses.

### Kcaliaz — 2012-03-16T02:35:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1185>

A really good way to get it in with no taste (my kids even eat it and have no idea!) is to get it ground into your grassfed ground beef. I purchase the "primal ground beef" from Slankers (can find them online). Great product and sooooo easy to eat liver!! Try it, you'll love it! In fact, I just ordered 15 more pounds of it today...

### rbu5@sssnet.com — 2012-03-16T02:49:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1200>

Liver is my favorite meal after steak! I use beef liver (soaked overnight in milk and rinsed well). I just cook it in bacon grease, salt and pepper it well. Sometimes I add onions if I am able to add extra carbs. Another yummy way is to wrap bacon around chicken livers and bake for 30 or so minutes till bacon is crispy in a large dish. I will make a pound of the livers and 1/2 lb bacon (half slices) and eat off this for days..usually 4 or 5 pieces per meal. I soak the chicken livers in a marinade of garlic powder, garlic salt, a little soy sauce and Worcestershire sauce to flavor.

### kalibex — 2012-03-16T03:25:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1229>

You might like to try chicken livers first - they're a bit 'milder', I find.

### sandy — 2012-03-16T04:36:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1268>

I just can't even make myself try it. I have to make it for hub once in awhile and its so gross. I do love liver sausage, and will eat that on some coconut "bread"

### Glamazon — 2012-03-16T05:05:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1280>

This is good. I found it on the net. It reminds me of liver sausage which is not as intensely flavored as just liver. I always think of pate as baby food for adults.

LisaÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s Luscious Liver Pate

1 pound liver, as free range and grass fed as possible, cut into pieces 

1/2 onion, chopped sea salt and pepper 

1 bunch parsley 

butter, coconut oil, olive oil, or organic lard 

seasonings of your choice: try an Italian blendÃ¢â‚¬Â¦herbes de ProvenceÃ¢â‚¬Â¦rosemary, garlic, thyme, sageÃ¢â‚¬Â¦ 

1\. Sautee onion in oil until translucent (5 min). Remove and place in blender. 

2\. Add more oil to the pan and add liver. 

3\. Sprinkle with salt, pepper and seasonings. 

4\. Cook on both sides. Inside should still be pink (few minutes on both sides). 

5\. Add liver AND JUICES FROM PAN to blender. If the cooking hasnÃ¢â‚¬â„¢t yielded much juice, add a splash of water.

 6. Blend. 

7\. Taste. It shouldnÃ¢â‚¬â„¢t taste overly Ã¢â‚¬Å“liveryÃ¢â‚¬Â. If it does, add more seasonings. 

8\. Eat on its own or with crackers, tortilla, celery, carrotÃ¢â‚¬Â¦ 

9\. Prepare for bionic strength!

[http://hartkeisonline.com/whole-and-natural-foods/liver-the-worlds-most-nutrient-dense-food-easy-liver-pate-recipe/](http://http://hartkeisonline.com/whole-and-natural-foods/liver-the-worlds-most-nutrient-dense-food-easy-liver-pate-recipe/)

### Lyndra — 2012-03-16T05:15:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1283>

> **Glamazon;1281 wrote:** This is good. I found it on the net. It reminds me of liver sausage which is not as intensely flavored as just liver. I always think of pate as baby food for adults.

Sounds good - I will have to give it a try.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-16T07:17:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1315>

We had this discussion on the MDA monster thread with everyone sharing their suggestions. It was concluded that lamb's liver and pig's liver are the mildest. MamaGrok eats one ounce raw (chopped up and quickly!) every morning. Shijin adds it to her burgers and neither her nor her kids mind. I buy high quality liverwurst from a local butcher (made of nothing but pig's liver, pig's head and herbs) and it's easy to eat, both cold and cooked (where as when I tried to cook chicken livers I almost puked). Dr K said to me to soak them in milk before hand if you do cook. I eat some every day because my K2 is low.

### Kiera2 — 2012-03-16T07:47:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1320>

I made meatballs with 1Kg beef mince and 500gm sheeps liver, with turmeric, cumin, maca, cinnamon, oragano. After they had been devoured, I told my 13yold there was liver in there and she said "Oh, that's a good way to get it into us. They're yum."

### caroline — 2012-03-16T09:19:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1334>

I make chicken liver pate a lot - I take it to parties etc as well. everyone loves it. It is very simple with onions, garlic, salt and pepper and butter and then put it in a food processor. I can post the recipe if anyone is nterested

### CoolingWeb — 2012-03-16T09:34:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1335>

Cumin. That is what you need.

250g Beef liver cut thin slices (Stroganoff). Add Salt and LOTS of Cumin (3-4 tea spoons or more) and some garlic (powder is fine). Mix well. I add red chili peppers flakes to make it HOT HOT

Put olive oil and butter in a skillet. When butter melts and skilit is hot add the liver.

Toss for few minutes until it gets dark.

(lately, I started adding the curcumin to it. Generous amounts. 2-4 tbs. I add it 1m before Its done.)

You are done.

Eat with steamed vegetables. Broccoli and such ...

Enjoy.

### Grottenolm — 2012-03-16T10:16:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1343>

Having never eaten liver while young, or prevously in my life, I would highly recommend lamb liver to start with. It tastes mild and sweet, and even milder than chicken overs in my opinion. Soak it in lemon juice for a few hours before cooking to draw out the bitter blood, and you will be sweet.

I like to mix it with lamb mince and make spicy meatball curry. Or just panfry thin strips on extremely High heat to seal, leaving the middle still pink. Serve with bacon and don't forget to deglaze the pan with cream or wine for an accompanying sauce  I love leaving the butcher with a cheap bag of organs that will let me eat like a queen.

### mtdejong1@gmail.com — 2012-03-16T13:01:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1360>

I mix it with ground meat and Italian herbs, then fry. Tastes good with ketchup.

### David — 2012-03-16T15:02:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1411>

Check out the information at the Weston Price site:

[http://www.westonaprice.org/food-features/liver-files](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/food-features/liver-files)

I've tried a few of these preparations and all seemed okay, so far.

### edance83@yahoo.com — 2012-03-16T17:37:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1510>

I hate all the liver I have tried so far, but I knew I needed to eat it. And I can actually afford it grassfed so I had to find a way.

This month I finally figured it out.

I use grassfed beef liver from WF. I soak it over night in milk. Then rinse it. I put it in a food processor and add salt, an onion, and bacon if I have it. Blend it until it is a paste. Then add it to my ground beef. Then I make the burgers however I want to. I love to add curry powder to my burgers, but I change it up often. I have extremely sensitive taste and smell.. so on occasion I get a faint hint of the taste of something "different" than beef, but it's not bad in any way. Most people I think wouldn't even know it was in there.

### Nan — 2012-03-16T22:22:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1685>

One key to great fried liver is to make sure the slices are thin, about 1/4 inch. Most store liver is about 1/2 inch thick, which means the marinade doesn't get through it. I marinate the liver in some white wine (milk, or rice wine or other light vinegar work too) overnight; coat with coconut flour, fry in bacon fat quickly, then top with fried onions and bacon. And for some reason, I don't think the meal is complete without turnip greens or other sharp greens as a side. I feel better when I eat liver.

### vkiernan — 2012-03-16T23:14:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1706>

I love fried beef liver and onions. I fry up my onions in butter then add the liver and fry it in amongst the onions till browned. But, don't overcook it. This grass-fed beef liver has been the best. I even eat it in the am with my eggs. Too bad one doesn't get more with a 1/4 beef. Will have to find some somewhere else. Quick, easy and delish.

### Birdy — 2012-03-16T23:55:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1728>

I like cooking it with bacon and onions.

### Shijin13 — 2012-03-17T00:39:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1748>

I buy lamb liver, puree it up, then mix it in w/grassfed/finished ground beef - and season w/ turmeric, cumin, cinnamon, black pepper, salt and garlic

On monday I made enough to last me 2 weeks...and then the kids wanted burgers for lunch - and then they wanted them again 2 days later!

no complaints that the kids are eating liver - liver is a heck of a lot cheaper than buying green pastures FCLO!

### ginastarke — 2012-03-17T03:28:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-1832>

Beef liver, I'm still getting the hang of cooking. Definitely giving grilling it a try when I spend some time traeger-sitting, oops I mean house sitting for my parents.

Chicken liver, whole other story. whenever I roast a chicken or two, I cook a little batch of pate for my mom and I to snack on while we cook and catch up. It's great spread on romaine leaves.

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-03-18T17:00:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-2386>

I love lamb's liver, fried in butter with bacon (and sometimes with onion) - that's a perfect meal for me. Chicken livers are great, too. A great way of having them is as a stuffing for a grilled portabello mushroom, with an additional topping of mashed cauliflower (mixed with a little cream cheese if you're eating dairy, or a little bit of coconut milk if you're not!). Another nice way of cooking them is to place sheets of baking parchment on a tray, make a little pile of finely chopped onions and leeks, top with thinly sliced liver and dripping, season, wrap into parcels and bake.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-03-18T17:49:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-2394>

I eat a slice of liverwurst or braunschwieger in the morning to eat with my hamburger. A bite of each together is pure heaven. So much better than just liver by itself. I also mix lamb liver with beef and beef bacon and lots of seasonings and spices for the burgers.

### wyldeman — 2012-04-05T08:05:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-11643>

I soak mine for a bit in some So Delicious brand coconut milk, then pan fry it in CO. Sometimes with onions. It's pretty simple, but I love it. Grass fed beef is very popular locally, but luckily for me, the liver is not....so it's readily available and cheap.

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-05T08:11:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-11646>

Making this tomorrow. Ultimate beef and liver chili. [http://www.mommypotamus.com/ultimate-beef-liver-chili/](http://http://www.mommypotamus.com/ultimate-beef-liver-chili/)

### Baba — 2012-04-05T18:41:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-11776>

I don't know if you can get this in the US, but duck liver and rabbit liver are my favourites!

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-05T19:27:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-11797>

> **Lyndra;1153 wrote: I haven't been able to bring myself to make liver, after a nasty experience years ago when I stunk up the house for days. I know it has all sorts of health benefits so I'd like to get to the point where I can tolerate it.
>
>
>
> Is there any "mild" form of liver? Better ways to prepare it so it won't smell so much? I bought some liverwurst at the deli one day, and it didn't taste bad, but I worry about what else is in it.**

**

Hi Lyndra,

I love liver and onions. The secret to cooking liver is to not overcook.** Pate is better for people trying to get to like liver. Chicken liver is the mildest. Here are two recipes:

[http://www.eatkamloops.org/liver-and-onions/](http://http://www.eatkamloops.org/liver-and-onions/)

[http://www.eatkamloops.org/perfect-pate/](http://http://www.eatkamloops.org/perfect-pate/)

I hope that helps some.

### ginastarke — 2012-04-07T19:29:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-12484>

> **oliviascotland;2393 wrote:** I love lamb's liver, fried in butter with bacon (and sometimes with onion) - that's a perfect meal for me. Chicken livers are great, too. A great way of having them is as a stuffing for a grilled portabello mushroom, with an additional topping of mashed cauliflower (mixed with a little cream cheese if you're eating dairy, or a little bit of coconut milk if you're not!). Another nice way of cooking them is to place sheets of baking parchment on a tray, make a little pile of finely chopped onions and leeks, top with thinly sliced liver and dripping, season, wrap into parcels and bake.

Thanks! Just bought a whole lamb's liver - the thing is the size of a pot roast! Any ideas for the leftovers?

I keep hearing this from my parents, probably CW, but, "You shouldn't have liver too often - it's very bad for you!"

If it's grass fed, I assume the pollution isn't an issue, so is the high level of vitamin A possibly a risky thing? Since I'm not eating any plant sources of B vitamins, I was hoping I could eat it at least once a week for the B-12.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-08T02:11:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-12553>

What I do when we slaughter a hog or lamb is to cut the liver into about 1-2c pieces. (My husband and I will each have about a cup for each meal.) I then freeze the liver pieces in individual bags and put all the individual bags in one big bag. I usually double bag organ meat so they will stay fresh longer in the freezer.

We grow our own hogs and most of our cattle but get our lamb from a local rancher that does not treat her pasture with anything but manure. Yes, liver can be toxic if the rancher sprays his pasture. I would ask the rancher about his practices before buying an animal.

Eat your liver twice a week. You will not overdue the fat solvable vitamins and your urine will have a rich yellow color.

### ginastarke — 2012-04-15T03:48:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-15196>

This is more on the primal side, since it has a little dairy and nightshades, but once I got the lamb liver of the grill, it tasted a bit stronger than expected and I made "gyros" with it: I sliced up the liver with cucumbers, onions and as little as I could manage red pepper and tomatoes , and made tzatziki with Greek yogurt, lemon juice, fresh mint garlic, cucumbers and pepper, which I also used as little as possible, and served in in romaine leaves. It was terrific!

### BeingVenus — 2012-04-16T19:54:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-15638>

I used to love liver but for some strange reason after pregnancy I just couldn't stomach it unless its in a pate. Since I'm too lazy to make

PÃ¢tÃ© except for Jewish holidays I just make a frozen liver pills, which is supposedely how Sally Fallon gets her liver in every day. In fact, I just chopped up a 1.5lb lamb liver in to little baggies and put it in the freezer this morning.

"The best way to eat raw liver is to freeze it for 14 days to avoid any parasitic infection (unlikely but possible especially in those with impaired immune systems). When frozen, cut it into teaspoon sized pieces and put them into little coin bags. Keep them in your freezer and use one baggie at at time, chopping into little pills to swallow with a drink. The primary benefit of swallowing liver pills frozen, is you canâ€™t taste it. I take my liver pills with raw milk for the extra nourishment and so I donâ€™t burp up the taste."

### Lyndra — 2012-06-23T05:25:44+0300

Thanks everybody for all the suggestions for preparation!

Then, I make small think pieces of liver. Once that is done, mix it all together and eat.

### Lyndra — 2012-06-23T18:39:57+0300

Sorry to threadjack, buuuut -my backyard remodel is almost complete. Have big ol planting beds now. Lyndra - did you do asparagus here in CO? Any ideas on how to grow it?

### Lyndra — 2012-06-24T01:23:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-do-you-or-dont-you.185/post-33229>

> **Cavemam;33332 wrote:** Sorry to threadjack, buuuut -my backyard remodel is almost complete. Have big ol planting beds now. Lyndra - did you do asparagus here in CO? Any ideas on how to grow it?

Yes, asparagus grows quite well but it needs good drainage, adequate water, and seems to attract weeds like bees to honey. It is planted in mid-spring, usually bare-root plants (so it's too late for this year). You'll be able to harvest a few spears the second year but not too many, otherwise you'll deplete the roots too much. For details on growing, see [http://www.ext.colostate.edu/mg/gardennotes/719.html#asparagus](http://http://www.ext.colostate.edu/mg/gardennotes/719.html#asparagus). I used to volunteer as a master gardener, so ask me about plants .

# Thread: How do you know your Leptin is reset? And how about relapse?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 71
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Petra — 2012-03-16T02:43:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-1191>

In January I did a strict reset. Did not lose weight, but I felt full quite fast and had no cravings for sugar and/or carbs.

Now with CT I get cravings again even for carbs. Did the reset not work out?

Lately I eat some 75-100grams of carbs a day, is that causing relapse and I'm getting Leptin Resistant again? How does that work?

Thanks already for your thoughts and input,

Petra

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-03-16T02:54:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-1206>

With CT, you're also supposed to be eating keto style and limiting carbs. With the reset, if you have weight to lose, you should stay under 50 grams of carbs/day...but with keto, it's much lower than that. If the carbs are making you crave, I would cut them out or limit them much more, adding in more fats instead. Just my two cents...

### Glamorama — 2012-03-16T02:58:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-1210>

There's a list on the blog about the signs to look out for ebfore you are fully LS. Doc says it takes 6-8 weeks, but often longer, depending on your health-status as you start. I have a feeling age matters too.

Having been there and done that (the banana-incident in nov 2011...), I'm tipping whats happening is that CT gets you hungry ebcause its burning fat. Our bodies' antural response to that is to ask to be fed by driving hunger. The fact that your each for carbs, to me indicates that you're not leptin sensitive at the liver (this was me for a long long time), OR you have Candida-overgroth in your gut. 75-100 grams carbs is a lot if its not summer, and you want to lose weight. I did 80-100 grams over summer, but now I'm down to 40-60 grams daily (fall here in Oz). By June/July I plan on being keto.

### Souldanzer — 2012-03-16T03:21:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-1223>

A nugget Dr. K posted in response to a question I had that's something to consider if things aren't working out with CT:

> @Souldanzer…….you have hit on something I have just mentioned in passing. The Ancient pathway is built for survival and longevity but one thing it does not protect us from is excessive CRH from light……..so just say you become a modern cold adapted hominid…….and you eat correctly…….but you use light from your modern technology at night……Well this is a major biologic mismatch that raises cortisol……….The Ancient pathway has no answer for this so it can destroy the effects that the Ancient pathway provides. You may realize now, why I was in the dark talking, to Sean Croxton on my underground wellness appearance………Many people have not picked this up but this is a major reason I am concerned about our modern paleo’s. Not only do they eat carbs at the wrong time they also use technology all the time including the wrong times.

### Glamorama — 2012-03-16T03:30:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-1236>

> **Souldanzer;1224 wrote:** A nugget Dr. K posted in response to a question I had that's something to consider if things aren't working out with CT:

I noticed CT made me sleepy, but then the TV would wake me up again! Planning on getting yellow lense glasses to wear at home for the times I want to watch a movie or stay up to watch a docu etc.

Petra

I'm 6 weeks into the Leptin Rx and really thought I had cravings beat. Do you guys really think it is lack of Leptin sensitivity in the liver? Or could it be something else?

And if cravings are happening to people, what do you do to cope?

### Petra — 2012-03-16T12:26:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-1355>

> **TheKid;1354 wrote:** Wow. I'm glad I'm not the only one facing cravings. I started CT about 2 weeks ago and seemingly out of nowhere cravings popped up this week. I'm 6 weeks into the Leptin Rx and really thought I had cravings beat. Do you guys really think it is lack of Leptin sensitivity in the liver? Or could it be something else?
>
>
>
> And if cravings are happening to people, what do you do to cope?

For me I write them off as looking for comfort now that I CTing. The cold is making me unconformable. I'm not there yet, that is why I fell back on some carbs again. Which is no good choice, might you consider giving in to the cravings. Brain fog is coming up.

It is definitely no hunger pang. So Jack's right, cold reduces hunger feelings, yet cravings are coming back and twisting my sobriety.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-03-16T14:50:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-1403>

For the cravings try upping your fat content during the day, I find mine are abolished if I eat 70+% fat in the day. it\`s really hard to want carbs with that level of fat in the diet.

### Croak — 2012-03-16T17:18:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-1493>

It's not looking for comfort, it's your body saying:

"HEY, I'M EATING YOU ALIVE HERE TO STAY WARM, YOU NEED TO THROW SOME LOGS ON THE FIRE IF YOU WANT TO KEEP WHAT YOU GOT!"

You're not craving carbs per se, you're just getting a strong hunger signal _after_ CT which is normal, and you are translating that to carb desire.

When the doc says CT kills hunger, he's right...no hunger at all when cold, but as you warm up that's a different matter.

### Groketteliveson — 2012-03-16T18:00:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-1539>

>

>
>
>
> When the doc says CT kills hunger, he's right...no hunger at all when cold, but as you warm up that's a different matter.

My hunger comes in phases. For the most part CT has cut the hunger cravings. Guess I need to start paying closer attention as to when the hunger is triggered.

### Dan in Utah — 2012-03-16T18:19:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-1553>

Hey Petra,

Here's my list of signs of leptin sensitivity:

• Less hunger and cravings

• Sleeping like a rock

• Feeling refreshed in the morning

• Less muscle soreness

• Mental calmness

• Steady weight loss

• Increased libido

• Softer skin and stronger nails

• Improved mental ability

• Fewer headaches

• Dreaming, especially about moving your body (dancing, exercising, exploring, etc.)

• More energy

• More sweating with exertion

• Firmer gums, smoother teeth

I guess a relapse would be a reverse of the above.

I crave carbs, too, at times, but much less than a few months ago. If someone's metabolism has been leptin resistance/insulin dominant, they're riding on a repeating 90-120 minute hyperglycemia/insulin surge/reactive hypoglycemia metabolic roller-coaster. Leptin will eventually tame insulin, but it can take awhile. I think adequate sleep, stress management, stopping inflammation, and CT are equally important as carb restriction. Eating seasonally make a difference, too. If your stuck, you need labs to guide your way out.

Good luck!

### mycrazycartoons@gmail.com — 2012-03-16T18:49:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-1583>

I'm 6 weeks into the Leptin Rx and really thought I had cravings beat. Do you guys really think it is lack of Leptin sensitivity in the liver? Or could it be something else?

>
> And if cravings are happening to people, what do you do to cope?

Same here... I thought I was losing it--to suddenly start having carb cravings after never even thinking about food for nearly 6 weeks. ### claudia rowe — 2012-03-17T12:48:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-1913>

> **WandaP;1701 wrote: I'm new, so I have a question. Is "keto" putting your body in ketosis?
>
>
> It is, yes.
>
>
>
> WandaP;1701 wrote:** What do you do to overcome bad breath that goes with ketosis?

It's awful, isn't it? I blend masses of fresh mint and parsley, then pass them through a sieve, add water and voila, a mouth wash. I swish with half a big glass and spit and swallow the remainder. It's delicious and does seem to work better than commercial mouth washes.

I also brush about 6 times a day with a very soft toothbrush (so as not to wear out the enamel).

### meyoolia@gmail.com — 2012-03-17T14:08:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-1928>

> **TheKid;1354 wrote:** Wow. I'm glad I'm not the only one facing cravings. I started CT about 2 weeks ago and seemingly out of nowhere cravings popped up this week. I'm 6 weeks into the Leptin Rx and really thought I had cravings beat. Do you guys really think it is lack of Leptin sensitivity in the liver? Or could it be something else?
>
>
>
> And if cravings are happening to people, what do you do to cope?

Yup. I don't have cravings. It's just ...simple... STARVATION! I'm pretty peckish by bedtime (even after eating a fatty supper and 4T worth of "squirrel candy" - mainly coconut oil, manna and some stevia) and especially now that I've dropped the tub temp a bit more, I'm RAVENOUS in the wee hours of the night. I ignore it, wait for breakfast, and wake up with a headache for a few days. Can't wait til this wild blood-sugar swinging settles down, but I'm noticing a trend: hunger goes with blood sugar swings, eventually goes with a bout of nausea and another weightloss and fasting blood sugar plateau busted. It's a good thing  (I hope?)

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-03-17T14:08:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-1929>

> **Dan in Utah;1555 wrote:** Hey Petra,
>
>
>
> Here's my list of signs of leptin sensitivity:
>
>
>
> • Less hunger and cravings
>
> • Sleeping like a rock
>
> • Feeling refreshed in the morning
>
> • Less muscle soreness
>
> • Mental calmness
>
> • Steady weight loss
>
> • Increased libido
>
> • Softer skin and stronger nails
>
> • Improved mental ability
>
> • Fewer headaches
>
> • Dreaming, especially about moving your body (dancing, exercising, exploring, etc.)
>
> • More energy
>
> • More sweating with exertion
>
> • Firmer gums, smoother teeth
>
>
>
> I guess a relapse would be a reverse of the above.
>
>
>
> I crave carbs, too, at times, but much less than a few months ago. If someone's metabolism has been leptin resistance/insulin dominant, they're riding on a repeating 90-120 minute hyperglycemia/insulin surge/reactive hypoglycemia metabolic roller-coaster. Leptin will eventually tame insulin, but it can take awhile. I think adequate sleep, stress management, stopping inflammation, and CT are equally important as carb restriction. Eating seasonally make a difference, too. If your stuck, you need labs to guide your way out.
>
>
>
> Good luck!

This is great stuff Dan! Simple markers to use. I believe in getting the eating right before you spend money on labs. Your markers are great indicators. I would say if you are not hitting 75% of your markers by 12 weeks get labs done.

### tellmisty — 2012-03-17T16:13:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-1979>

As someone who didn't get labs for three months ... I don't think one should wait. I can't believe the stuff we saw on my labs ... so many things I tweaked to really start reversing some stuff and getting healthy. I wish I would have done it straight out of the gate; I feel like I lost time needlessly, and reversing things takes months, so it was important time.

Just my two cents.

### Cody — 2012-03-22T19:38:08+0200

My legs are all itchy and yeasty, but they are already better after just 3 weeks on the leptin reset. (I need to up my protein at breakfast, still. So hard to reach 50 grams of protein!)

Try drinking more water. A lot more water. I have no issues with keto breath, but I drink over a gallon per day.

### Petra — 2012-03-23T11:09:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-4887>

Thanks Dan for your check list.

As I look at it and put it to the test this week, I feel confident leptin is working for me.

Then there was this reply of Croak"

It's not looking for comfort, it's your body saying:

"HEY, I'M EATING YOU ALIVE HERE TO STAY WARM, YOU NEED TO THROW SOME LOGS ON THE FIRE IF YOU WANT TO KEEP WHAT YOU GOT!"

Makes sense right?

Then there comes CT-7 in which I read that the fat burned during CT releases that much leptin, that cravings and hunger should be reduced.

So before I release too much cortisol. I think I will leave it like that. I really like the cold showers and working in cold/cool environments. My eating habits and cravings, ok, I can deal with it... We get triggered many times a day to eat or drink. I just hoped it would all go away, and my mind would get more clear. Petra

### claudia rowe — 2012-03-26T16:08:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-6320>

I'm wondering how long it takes, also. I'm at the end of week 8, have stuck to the Leptin Rx plan religiously and my serum leptin levels are the worst they've ever been. Actually, all my bloods are out of whack including free T3, rT3, DHEA and cortisol. Actually, these are the worst bloods I've had in 8 years except that my lupus antibodies are slightly lower. Even my cholesterol, normally pretty low, is higher than usual.

The MS related neck tremor is significantly better, almost completely gone.

I'm frankly a little concerned. After 8 weeks strict paleo, supplements, water, CT and only eating BAB and then coconut oil for afternoon tea, I've gained 4kg and my bloods have seemingly gone backwards.

I'm mystified!!

Have you had your sex steroid hormones tested? When you are gaining weight and having cravings it's usually a sign that your hormones are out of whack. I did great the first six weeks. Then started CT and the cravings and hunger started and then the weight came on. I gained 6# back. Then I got tested and sure enough my hormones were WAY out of whack. It sounds like there are a lot of good things going on for you so there are other issues still present in the body. Check hormones!

### Destiny — 2012-03-27T01:39:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-your-leptin-is-reset-and-how-about-relapse.189/post-6661>
