Y'know, sometimes I think we get sucked into the mindset of thinking "If it's good, more is better!" If you go back to the CT protocol blog, Dr. Kruse says we should plan on doing the baths "2-5 times a week depending upon your starting weight, BF %, and what your goals are." I don't think we should necessarily be doing the baths daily.

Your fatigue is probably your body asking for a little rest : )

### tweety — 2012-03-24T19:55:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-before-the-ct-mayhem-ends.193/post-5500>

Good point. I have been taking it slow, no shivering but I still feel like I am making progress. I had a few days of intense muscle tiredness, and I backed off a little and I am back tp feeling better.

As a side note, I am so so so thankful for CT. I had been on the reset since last summer and my husband was a very cranky, unwilling and stubborn supporter and sometimes participant. I told him about CT a few weeks ago, which I thought he would balk at, and he embraced it entirely! This morning, on his own accord, he started counting all his protein grams trying to get his BAB! Thats enough success for me, I am happy! Thanks Dr. K!

### Claudia — 2012-03-24T19:59:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-before-the-ct-mayhem-ends.193/post-5505>

> **Swagger;5360 wrote:** It's not just you ladies. I'm a hormonal, emotional bastard of a man 15 days into this. I will never say a bad word about PMS ever again.

Hysterical! I love this, too, and feel the same way : "Happiness is a bag of ice and a jar of coconut oil...what did I just say???"

### Claudia — 2012-03-25T04:41:14+0300

I was eating a lot of bison last year and then, I discovered it was not grass-fed when I contacted the company to ask about it and found more info on its website that they supplemented with grain. That might have increased my 06 level a bit.

# Thread: Hello from Long Island, NY
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-long-island-ny.199/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaleoDentist — 2012-03-16T13:58:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-long-island-ny.199/post-1373>

Hi everyone!

I've been doing Paleo since 2001 after reading Cordain's first book. fell off the wagon 3 times and regained all the weight, this is my 4th go around. I'm 5'9 medium frame went form 225lb to 184 in about a year eating pretty strict Paleo. Robb Wolf's Paleo Solution was my kick in the butt Feb 2011. while I fall off the wagon occasionally (chocolate is my gateway drug). I am now working my way through all of jacks blog posts from the beginning. looking forward to learning about CT. I am not sure if I am Leptin Sensitive. I look pretty good, but still have a bit of body fat left. (although my wife tells me I do not need to loose any more fat as I will look too skinny) On weekdays I eat only dinner until I am full and I am a bit afraid I eat too little, but I want to trust my body and listen to my satiety so I stop when full). So I think I am leptin sensitive, however when I go off Paleo for any extended time I gain fat fast. (7 day cruise with eating bread and dessert at dinners and 3 meals a day I gained 7 lbs, but lost it all in 2-3 weeks after going back to my normal eating routine) on weekends I eat 2-3 times a day more for social reasons.

I am inspired by Jacks Passion not only for optimizing health , but for his commitment to spreading this message.

As a dentist practicing 15 years, I have noticed the declining health of my patients. its scary. Having seen me lose weight and look better my patients constantly ask me what I am doing. I gove them a list of books and blogs to read, including Jacks!

Welcome!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

# Thread: Labs for Leptin Insensitivity
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaleoDentist — 2012-03-16T14:02:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/post-1377>

Hi, can someone please list the labs Dr Kruse recommends to test for leptin insensitivity/sensitivity. Please include the name and the ideal range. and the non ideal. I know its in the blogs, but I am sure some one wrote them down. Thanks!

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-16T16:21:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/post-1446>

I think you're looking for this: [Optimizing labs](http://http://jackkruse.com/what-are-the-optimizing-labs/)

How did I find it? [Here](http://https://docs.google.com/document/pub?id=1lhFLT8y0Lhn4KVX9B7YNJNRbduLr7Z_6bme_8E-XYOo). Bookmark that last link especially.

### Dan in Utah — 2012-03-16T19:18:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/post-1606>

Leptin resistance means you'll have high leptin in the face of low VIP, low MSH, and low AVP levels, not to mention a host of other mismatched hormone signals: low GnRH, low growth hormone, etc. These are pretty expensive tests, unfortunately.

I think this is part of why Dr. Kruse suggests the mirror test: If you look in the mirror and your pudgy...well, you know.

I'd love to order all these test on my obese patients, but most people don't want to spend that kind of money, yet labs really do help if someone is really sick or horribly stalled.

Here are some cheap tests that give you a clue: low HDL, high TGs, high A1c, high PM cortisol, low vitamin D, low DHEA, low magnesium, high HS-CRP (in my limited experience anyway). Insurance will cover these pretty well most of the time and they're not too pricey. Hope that helps.

### vlynnb — 2012-03-18T23:23:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/post-2473>

Dan, I know everyone's insurance differs but mine is a high deductible. catastrophic policy so I am not sure it would cover these tests. Does there usually have to be a certain diagnosis to justify these tests or will a routine yearly physical usually justify them?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-03-19T02:31:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/post-2525>

you can get a Leptin level or a T3 and rev T3 and then use the conversion calculator at the STTM.com to see where your ratio is. We like it above 20. Other signs......high LDL levels because of a low T3 conversion to pregnenolone. high ferritin, haptoglobin, HS CRP, low Vit D and low DHEA levels.......all things that walk hand and hand with LR.

### L8F — 2012-03-19T02:32:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/post-2526>

The most bang for your buck comes with HS CRP, DHEAS and Vit D. You can learn a lot from those numbers. Also for women, a day 21 Progesterone and Estradiol are helpful.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-03-19T02:33:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/post-2528>

> **L8F;2533 wrote:** The most bang for your buck comes with HS CRP, DHEA and Vit D. You can learn a lot from those numbers. Also for women, a day 21 Progesterone and Estradiol are helpful.

Lucy knows the deal now.......she heard it live from our mouths.

I do not know if this company is nationwide but they are in Fl. You can get just about any test without a dr. referral.

I would think that you could walk in and get pricing for all the tests the doc recommends.

### meyoolia@gmail.com — 2012-03-19T21:39:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/post-2910>

> **Jack Kruse;2535 wrote:** Lucy knows the deal now.......she heard it live from our mouths.

Great! I just had them done recently myself - so now how do we interpret the results? High/Low/ok indicates what?

And btw, it's SO reassuring to know you're still looking in!

### L8F — 2012-03-19T23:37:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/post-2957>

Post the results here with ranges, and people will comment...

Basicly you want high Vit D, meaning at least 50, Dr K likes 70s+. Others out there vary in what they prefer. Depends what is wrong with you etc.

DHEA(s) should be high. Example, mine was VERY low in the 70s. This is NOT optimal. You want MUCH higher. I forgot to mention the preferred lab is DHEAS which is a better more stable number than just plain DHEA which is also measurable.

There is also a whole science of relating Cortisol (Saliva via ZRT labs or thru Canary Club) and its relation to DHEA. Stop the Thyroid Madness also has more on COrtisol results.

HS or Cardio CRP is a marker for general body inflammation. Higher than 3 puts you in the higher risk cardiac category, but also is not good from a general health standpoint. That being said, acute ailments or issues can spot raise the CRPs. Unless you get the sensitive or Cardio CRP it will not be as helpful. It should be as close to zero as possible. Mine went down with strict AI Paleo protocol, and I am looking forward to retesting now that I have been ketogenic AI paleo pretty strictly for a few weeks, plus on/off CT.

As for E and P, it is important stuff. Progesterone is a range, and it should be nicely up in last half of cycle. 10 is low, for example. The E to P ratio in second half can also tell you a bunch about the mystical "Estrogen Dominance" which just means you have too much Estrogen circulating around. This is usually, though not always coupled with a too low amount of progesterone. =general imbalance. Too much E mucks around with all the other hormones and their functions=badness.

Vkiernan here knows a lot about the supps etc to work on this, and I think she has a good doc advising her. DIM is one, as is Milk Thistle which supposrts the liver working overtime on all this E processing. Cold also is supposed to help.

So there are my thoughts. HS CRP to get overall inflm, D for immune status and propping up all the rest, DHEA for hormonal status, and female hormones to get more down the line in the hormone cascade.

### Snowyky — 2012-03-20T00:14:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/post-2969>

Ok, I just have to say this, I love you guys  I've also been trying to figure out what to do about labs, the spreadsheet was a fantastic help, also your info L8F, I think it's doable now too, so I'll post my results when I get them back.

### L8F — 2012-03-20T00:37:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/post-2977>

I think the key is, unless you have a good Paleo type or BHRT doc to interpret things for you, to stick to basics as you implement all the Paleo diet, as well as any AI diets and LR stuff. Then you can retest reasonably cheaply the main markers to see the direction you are headed. If after some time things don't feel right, or you are at a body fat % plateau etc, then dig deeper.

The thing I have learned is that there is SO much going on in us, and you can delve as deeply as you want. How deep do you want to go,and how much of a difference will it make?? Not sure, but if you have AI issues, it doesn't get much better than strict Paleo AI diet, and maybe ketogenic.

### differentstory — 2012-03-20T22:15:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/post-3489>

> **L8F;2533 wrote:** The most bang for your buck comes with HS CRP, DHEAS and Vit D. You can learn a lot from those numbers. Also for women, a day 21 Progesterone and Estradiol are helpful.

Thank you for that! I was just looking to get my labs done but didn't want to spend $2,000 (yikes!). I did get the spreadsheet from Shijin13 - that was super helpful! But I'm feeling clueless as to what priority each test is. Besides those 5 labs you mentioned, I've read that reverse t3 is important and EVERYONE here seems to talk about cholesterol a lot and horomones. What would be the next set of high-priority tests?

Also, just wanted to add, I am completely clueless as to all of this. So sorry if I'm asking stupid questions!

### L8F — 2012-03-21T02:47:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/post-3693>

> **differentstory;3514 wrote:** Thank you for that! I was just looking to get my labs done but didn't want to spend $2,000 (yikes!). I did get the spreadsheet from Shijin13 - that was super helpful! But I'm feeling clueless as to what priority each test is. Besides those 5 labs you mentioned, I've read that reverse t3 is important and EVERYONE here seems to talk about cholesterol a lot and horomones. What would be the next set of high-priority tests?
>
>
>
> Also, just wanted to add, I am completely clueless as to all of this. So sorry if I'm asking stupid questions!

Well, what are your symptoms? Do you think that you could have thyroid issues? If so, get TSH, Free T3, Free T4, and Reverse T3. Also Cholesterol is also a good one to get. I would just go regular fasting cholesterol panel. Or, you could just go the basics, find out what those say, and go from there. I think a tight diet will help a ton of stuff, and it is what you would probably need to do anyway if there were derangements in stuff.

So, do the basics, see what is up there. Also symptoms help a lot in knowing what to test....Save your $ is that is not in surplus, and get super good diet going and test the basics...

I think that should be a good start until I can find a great Paleo doctor in my area.

Mine went down with strict AI Paleo protocol, and I am looking forward to retesting now that I have been ketogenic AI paleo pretty strictly for a few weeks, plus on/off CT.
>
> As for E and P, it is important stuff. Progesterone is a range, and it should be nicely up in last half of cycle. 10 is low, for example. The E to P ratio in second half can also tell you a bunch about the mystical "Estrogen Dominance" which just means you have too much Estrogen circulating around. This is usually, though not always coupled with a too low amount of progesterone. =general imbalance. Too much E mucks around with all the other hormones and their functions=badness.
>
> Vkiernan here knows a lot about the supps etc to work on this, and I think she has a good doc advising her. DIM is one, as is Milk Thistle which supposrts the liver working overtime on all this E processing. Cold also is supposed to help.
>
>
>
> So there are my thoughts. HS CRP to get overall inflm, D for immune status and propping up all the rest, DHEA for hormonal status, and female hormones to get more down the line in the hormone cascade.

Thanks so much for the response. My husband originally weighted in at 400 2 years ago. He's lost 100 pounds all my himself, but weight loss always (always always) stalls at 300. I really really want to help him.

Me on the other hand. . .\*sigh\*. I am a mess. Very poor diet for the past 5 years. I started a slow carb diet about 16 days ago and was hungry and generally miserable but started losing (lost about 4 pounds). Then I switched to paleo/leptin reset 12 days ago. I feel awesome. No cravings. Each day gets better and better but I am stuck with the weight loss.  I gained all 4 pounds back and haven't lost anything.

Prior to all of this, I was speculating there was something wrong with my horomones/thyroid.

Any help you can provide would be awesome. At this point I am lost. I am determined not to give up, but am frustrated today (scale was up again!! 5 straight days in a row.)

### chrystin.comeau@live.com — 2012-03-22T00:07:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/post-4097>

> Prior to all of this, I was speculating there was something wrong with my horomones/thyroid.
>
>
>
> Any help you can provide would be awesome. At this point I am lost. I am determined not to give up, but am frustrated today (scale was up again!! 5 straight days in a row.)

I'm LOL ing because when I first complained about gaining on the Leptin Reset, Dr. K piped in and said that _if you are gaining weight on the Rx, your hormones are out of whack_. Honestly, I thought "Whatever. This guy doesn't know me. I don't have menstrual/fertility/ovarian issues! My hormones are fine!" Fine if you don't see the need for estrogen or progesterone, LOL.

### L8F — 2012-03-22T01:26:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/post-4123>

> **cjco;4123 wrote:** I'm LOL ing because when I first complained about gaining on the Leptin Reset, Dr. K piped in and said that _if you are gaining weight on the Rx, your hormones are out of whack_. Honestly, I thought "Whatever. This guy doesn't know me. I don't have menstrual/fertility/ovarian issues! My hormones are fine!" Fine if you don't see the need for estrogen or progesterone, LOL.

Different Story: Take a look back at the original monster thread at some of this biggest successes on here now. ALL of them struggled big time with the reset. It is NOT a rapid weight loss strategy, but a get your health back and watch your weight normalize kind of thing. IT WORKS!!!! Especially if your hormones are all messed up (they are, almost everyone on here has messed up hormones and thinks they don't at first) Stay with it. It is simple to do, but not easy. I cannot remember who are the weight gainers initially, but there are many of them out there. Keep going, spend some time in the success stories area for inspiration.

### Dan in Utah — 2012-03-23T00:32:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-leptin-insensitivity.200/post-4682>

> **vlynnb;2480 wrote:** Dan, I know everyone's insurance differs but mine is a high deductible. catastrophic policy so I am not sure it would cover these tests. Does there usually have to be a certain diagnosis to justify these tests or will a routine yearly physical usually justify them?

It totally depends on the insurance and your plan. Usually a doc can justify the labs with symptom codes like fatigue, weight gain, insomnia, etc.

I'm still getting my feet wet with some of these labs, so I would defer to what Dr. Kruse might offer. I'm glad to see he's lurking in the forum now.

The effect is cumulative.

Follow the protocol to reach cold adaption, then reduce your bath to once a week along with daily ice packs and nightly sleeping on the cold mattress.

### Groketteliveson — 2012-03-28T16:15:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-adapted-mattress-pad.201/post-7698>

I received the cool/heat sleep pad yesterday. The unit is relatively quiet. You hear air pushing but it sounds something similar to a ceiling fan. As far as how cold the mattress gets, not very cold. I turned the cooling unit down to 50 and the unit never reached that. I never did receive the second one I ordered so I could compare the two. Thought maybe there was an issue with the pad itself and then I pulled out the manual and read this:

IMPORTANT Thermoelectric performance is a function of ambient room temperature. For example, if the room temperature is 80 F, the cool-heat sleep pad can cool down to 62 F(18'degrees difference). So at 80 F room temperature, if you set the powere unit to 55 F, the sleep pad will cool to 62 F.

So that being said, my room temp was 64 F. I had the unit set to 50 F and it never even reached past 60 F. So I called the company and they were good about sending a shipping label so I could pack it up and exchange for another unit. If the next one behaves the same way then I will just return it for good. But if you wanted to cool down while sleeping I think it would be good. The goal for me is to get my skin tempeture down and I don't think this pad is designed for that.

I'm guessing the seat pad is the same way.

> So that being said, my room temp was 64 F. I had the unit set to 50 F and it never even reached past 60 F. So I called the company and they were good about sending a shipping label so I could pack it up and exchange for another unit. ### Lyndra — 2012-05-29T06:30:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-adapted-mattress-pad.201/post-28139>

I bought one of these [http://www.solutions.com/1/1/3334-cool-gelmat.html](http://http://www.solutions.com/1/1/3334-cool-gelmat.html) in an effort to sleep through my hot flashes. It definitely helps cool off my body. When I roll over from one side to the other, I can feel heat radiating off the other side. The downside is it has a weird feel to it - you will definitely know you are sleeping on something. I've tried it underneath the top sheet and it still feels strange, but not quite as much as when lying directly on it. It will not make you icy cold but it does a reasonable job of cooling.

> **Anna K.;29316 wrote:** anybody else tried it?

Yes, I have been using mine since the end of March. It does cool me down at night, but is nowhere near being a substitute for an ice vest (which I also use daily, when possible) and I believe the pad cannot approach an ice-cold bath for effective CT. However, I imagine that I am getting chronic cold exposure, at least. A definite plus is that I seem to need less A/C. Also, my GF uses hers to HEAT and reports that it is much more effective than an electric blanket.

# Thread: Lipid Panel Question
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lipid-panel-question.202/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JeffieJoe — 2012-03-16T15:10:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lipid-panel-question.202/post-1414>

I don't have perfect health... that's what I'm here for  I've been eating paleo for a couple of years and recently started the LR and have been doing CT for a couple of weeks. Little more background, I completed a round of HCG in January and in the middle of Dec I had labs drawn. My Dr. had requested new labs because 6 months prior my cholesterol was too high for her liking. She told me the same 'ol Western Medicine advice: eat low fat and reduce my calories and exercise. So, my diet a couple weeks prior to the draw was zero fat and very low carb. However, my labs were worse then they were 6 months earlier. Here's where I'd like some advice as I'm not too concerned with cholesterol, however, I do have a lot of heart disease in my family and don't want to completely ignore the figures either.

Cholesterol Total: 266

Triglycerides: 76

HDL: 87

VLDL: 15

LDL: 164

C-Reactive: .74

Little more history. 1 1/2 years ago I was diagnosed with adrenal fatigue as my cortisol numbers are very low. They are coming back and I'm hoping with CT I'll be able to heal them completely. However, currently if I don't take my supplements I can barely make it through the day. I do know that your body will make extra cholesterol to try to compensate for the low cortisol.

Any thoughts??? I have to return to the Dr next week and am preparing myself for her to chew me out for not dieting and exercising enough. Oh, BTW... I'm 5'2" weigh 138 and have more muscle than most women.

### Shijin13 — 2012-03-16T15:31:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lipid-panel-question.202/post-1422>

Your cholesterol isnt' bad at all... your HDL is great, your LDL is fine... I'd worry about your trigs... my first question would be - were the LDL's a direct measure or calculated? that coupled w/your HDL and your trigs - can skew it... your CRP - was it a hs CRP? you really want to get your hsCRP as low as posible

most likely since you've got cortisol issues - you've got pregnenolone steal occurring - your cortisol problems - tell your liver to make more ldl - so it can convert it to pregnenolone and then use it to convert to the sex hormones - but b/c you've got cortisol issues - the pregnenolone is being shunted to support cortisol production. this in turn will turn off your hypothamlus -pituary and thyroid off - basically you're body is just idling and can't get in gear. its functioning just enough thats it.

I'd ask for a full sex hormone panel (Pregnenolone, alderstone, testosterone, progesterone, hs Estridol, total estrogens, prolactin, free T3, Reverse T3, Thyroid antibodies (both TPO and AB), DHEA-S, IGF-1, homocysteine, hs CRP, fasting blood glucose and fasting insulin

then you can begin to see the full picture.

the following link on MDA is Griff's Cholesterol primer - read it and figure out what your ratios are - that's a better indicator of what's going on...[http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread4723.html](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread4723.html)

additionally - you can always ask for a CIMT - which is a scan of the carotid arteries - which will tell you if you have any thickening, plaque etc - and is a better indicator of potential heart issues.

fwiw - a history of FHC (Familial hyper-cholesterol ) while it scares the crap out of CW - in the hands of a doc knowledgeable about lipids - will just go ok - you're mom/dad/brother/sister/aunt/grandfather etc have FHC - and no cardiac events - ok... no biggie, and then they'll just monitor you closely... one things you want to do is avoid statins - they aren't beneficial to women at all... and recent research is showing they can degrade the health of women....

### JeffieJoe — 2012-03-18T14:17:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lipid-panel-question.202/post-2348>

I really appreciate your insight. I read Griff's information a couple of months ago... great stuff! And, yes, when I use the Iranian formula my overall score is much better.

I do have a couple of questions though. You said you would be more concerned about triglycerides. Are you suggesting that mine are too high, or that is where I should focus my concern. I thought a score under a 100 for triglycerides was good??

Also, I will get a copy of my latest hormone panel. If there is a pregnenolone steal how does one go about fixing that, or will CT eventually right everything? I didn't mention that I currently take bio-identical progesterone on days 14-28.

Thanks so much for all you help!

### Shijin13 — 2012-03-19T15:17:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lipid-panel-question.202/post-2711>

If I remember correctly DR K likes to Trigs below 60

Pregnenolone steal - needs to focus on fixing the adrenals, and the use of the hormones... CT will work...but there are other ways... post what labs you've got and we'll get some more ppl to hack them...


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: How do you feel right before getting in the tub?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-right-before-getting-in-the-tub.203/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Evo Mama — 2012-03-16T15:20:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-right-before-getting-in-the-tub.203/post-1418>

I ask because so far, every time, my heart is beating really hard and I feel a bit of anxiety, I suppose about the initial plunge (which is really not that bad - it's my mind that thinks it will be worse than it actually is). I know Dr. K said that the body can warm up in anticipation of a cold bath, but I don't think that this (high HR) is what he meant.

I'm concerned that this is causing a nighttime spike in my cortisol. Once I'm in the bath, my HR slows down and bit, and by the time I'm done, I'm perfectly calm. Most of the time, I'm so relaxed I don't really even want to get out. I end my sessions because it's late and I need to get to sleep, more so than for any other reason.

I should mention I've only done 3 ice baths so far, so I suspect this response to getting in will diminish in intensity, but I wanted to see what others think re: cortisol and if that could be an issue. I should also mention I have no trouble falling asleep after bath.

Has anyone else been experiencing this? What are your experiences prior to taking the plunge?

What do ya'll think about the possible cortisol surge? Problematic or no biggie since I calm down after I'm in the bath for a few minutes?

Any thoughts would be GREATLY appreciated. Have I said lately how much I love this board?

### LisaAPB — 2012-03-16T15:27:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-right-before-getting-in-the-tub.203/post-1420>

I don't have a tub so I've been doing showers for almost 4 weeks, and I still get some anxiety and have to consciously make myself relax instead of clench up. I keep reading of people "loving" it, but that hasn't happened for me so far. I love the feeling afterwards though! In the hours after a cold shower, I feel just awesome. I've been using a wrapped cold pack on my back and tummy too, on the drive to work, and that is no problem whatsoever.

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-16T15:41:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-right-before-getting-in-the-tub.203/post-1423>

I'm not having the anxiety you're describing, but I do really have to make myself do it. Sort of like mentally prep for it and just do it. Once I'm in, I'm good for 45 min or more because, like you've said, it's relaxing. I would hope that the relaxation would counter any cortisol spike the anxiety might cause, but of course am not sure. Sounds like a good question for Dr. Kruse. I expect this will get easier as your body adjusts and realizes there is benefit to the cold water. I've found that if I read a little of the CT protocol posts or their comments before I get in that it steels my resolve to take a CT bath. Maybe that would calm your anxiety about it somewhat? Or you could always go the desensitization route and go back to face dunks and/or spot-icing, _then_ get in the bath, say, within minutes of each other. The mental system shock would be over by the time you lowered yourself into the water?

### Groketteliveson — 2012-03-16T16:13:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-right-before-getting-in-the-tub.203/post-1440>

I don't have the anxiety either but I also have to get myself mentally prepared. I'm more of the hot bath kind of gal, never really take showers just hot baths. It's been a real adjustment for me. Sure wish I enjoyed the cold baths like I enjoy the hot ones! I try not to think about it too much and just try to get it done daily. Been trying to get it done twice a day but sometimes I just don't have the mental energy knowing what I have to go through.

### MartiD — 2012-03-16T16:20:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-right-before-getting-in-the-tub.203/post-1443>

I actually look forward to them now...sick I know! LOL But I feel really good for several hours afterwards so I think that is the reason. I do a 10-15 minute in the morning and then a longer one at night but I do it early enough that I have at least two hours before I go to bed because I do have lots of energy after. I do sleep good as long as I don't take the bath to late. I have been doing them 3 weeks and my recovery time is much shorter...last night I didn't think my legs and butt were cold when I got out so dh felt them and said I was freezing. It felt like a sauna in our house when I came out. I had no feelings of being cold at all afterwards which is new just in last fews days. Of course it might be due to exceptionally warm weather here and the house is warm.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-03-16T16:26:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-right-before-getting-in-the-tub.203/post-1452>

I have found a big difference with showers vs. soaks. I think showers are harder! Plus, I'm a demon with my infrared thermometer and it never gets my skin to the zone (50-55), even when coming out at 45. Seems like just enough time between drops for skin to heat back up.

On the upside, I've lost weight before using CW and the jillian-michaels exercise crazyness. It's nowhere near as awful as heading into another 2 hr workout.

I am going by the "this is a step to adapted" and assuming it won't be so horrid once I get there!

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-16T16:28:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-right-before-getting-in-the-tub.203/post-1454>

> **Cavemam;1454 wrote:** I have found a big difference with showers vs. soaks. I think showers are harder! Plus, I'm a demon with my infrared thermometer and it never gets my skin to the zone (50-55), even when coming out at 45. Seems like just enough time between drops for skin to heat back up.
>
>
>
> On the upside, I've lost weight before using CW and the jillian-michaels exercise crazyness. It's nowhere near as awful as heading into another 2 hr workout.
>
>
>
> I am going by the "this is a step to adapted" and assuming it won't be so horrid once I get there!

Good point, Cavemam! I've been through the hours and hours of exercise to get results too, and I'd take a few minutes of 'mental prep' and seconds of sucking in breath over that chronic exercise ordeal any day.

### Evo Mama — 2012-03-16T16:59:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-right-before-getting-in-the-tub.203/post-1478>

Thanks, all!

I'm going to start reading a bit of the CT blog series before my bath (I printed them out so I can read them by candlelight in my womancave). I think I'll also to try the face dunk to see if that helps calm me down. Hopefully, the relaxation in tub is cancelling the cortisol surge I feel before getting in.

### Croak — 2012-03-16T17:02:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-right-before-getting-in-the-tub.203/post-1482>

Don't do the plunge then. Get in the empty tub and start filling with you in it. I think you'll find that's much less of a shock, especially if you're early in the adaptation process.

You can take the edge off even further by having an half-inch or so hot water in the tub when you start adding the cold. The hot water tends to float over the denser cold water, so it acts as a buffer as the water level rises, but eventually reaches equilibrium.

### Evo Mama — 2012-03-16T17:03:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-right-before-getting-in-the-tub.203/post-1483>

> **Croak;1484 wrote:** Don't do the plunge then. Get in the empty tub and start filling with you in it. I think you'll find that's much less of a shock, especially if you're early in the adaptation process.
>
>
>
> You can take the edge off even further by having an half-inch or so hot water in the tub when you start adding the cold. The hot water tends to float over the denser cold water, so it acts as a buffer as the water level rises, but eventually reaches equilibrium.

Thanks. I'll give this a try today. Smart thinking, Croak.

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-16T17:16:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-right-before-getting-in-the-tub.203/post-1491>

> **Croak;1484 wrote:** Don't do the plunge then. Get in the empty tub and start filling with you in it. I think you'll find that's much less of a shock, especially if you're early in the adaptation process.
>
>
>
> You can take the edge off even further by having an half-inch or so hot water in the tub when you start adding the cold. The hot water tends to float over the denser cold water, so it acts as a buffer as the water level rises, but eventually reaches equilibrium.

This is the method I used initially and I must say it really, really helped make the start of the whole thing a little easier, now that I look back. I've since abandoned it but notice that stepping into a few inches of cold water as it fills still takes my breath away for a second or two and not so much when I sit down and then begin running water in.

### Lyndra — 2012-03-16T17:28:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-right-before-getting-in-the-tub.203/post-1501>

Part of my anxiety involves worrying about doing this long term. Once I get in the tub, it's not so bad but I beforehand, when I think I'll be doing this every day for who knows how long, it's more daunting. I've decided it's like changing any kind of habit - think about doing it just this one time; not tomorrow, next week, etc. One day at a time. That's how I changed my eating habits. I didn't focus on what I could never eat again, I'd just think about what I was doing at this meal.

### Chrisgraff — 2012-03-16T17:45:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-right-before-getting-in-the-tub.203/post-1521>

Anyone tried adding ice after getting in the tub? Seems like it would be less of a shock to do it that way.

Edit: I didn't see Croak's comment. Ditto what he said!

### MJ* — 2012-03-16T18:03:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-right-before-getting-in-the-tub.203/post-1541>

> **Evo Mama;1420 wrote:** I ask because so far, every time, my heart is beating really hard and I feel a bit of anxiety, I suppose about the initial plunge (which is really not that bad - it's my mind that thinks it will be worse than it actually is). I know Dr. K said that the body can warm up in anticipation of a cold bath, but I don't think that this (high HR) is what he meant.

Did you start with the face plunging? There is a reason for the protocol...and for the record I haven't started the cold therapy except for cooling my showers to build up to it, and I'm reading a lot...with the face plunge in ice water, you active the dive reflex and that will lower your heart rate, maybe that could help reduce your anxiety some once you are ready to get in the tub. I like the idea of starting in an inch of warm water in the tub too and adding water while you are in it - it's less of a shock, plus you could gradually get used to it, make it cool for a couple of day, then get colder over time and eventually add ice, and you can wear neoprene socks and a hat while you adjust to doing this...

### Birdy — 2012-03-16T18:06:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-right-before-getting-in-the-tub.203/post-1543>

I am already a swimmer and go through this whenever I get into the water. Just dive right in. If I have to think of anything, I think of how hard it was to be on a cw low-fat reducing diet, and then a wave of relief passes over me. I think of it more like shiatsu. It hurts good, and nothing beats good sleep.

### Evo Mama — 2012-03-17T00:49:15+0200

Yes, I started with face plunges, so I've been basically following the protocol. I agree that's key.

### Evo Mama — 2012-03-17T00:52:13+0200

Okay, today I ate sashimi, did face plunges for 10 minutes, filled the tub about 4 inches, got in and filled the rest of the way. My heart rate was up a little, but nothing like before. Thanks!

# Thread: Post workout carb eating?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 36
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaleoDentist — 2012-03-16T17:01:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-1479>

It seems a common recommendation/ practice to eat more carbs following weight training to replenish depleted muscle glycogen stores. What happens when we do not do that and eat low carb instead??? how does the glycogen get restored? does it come form protein via gluconeogenesis? from fat? Is it a BAD idea not to eat a more carby meal following LHT (Lift heavy things) exercise. This is for those of us trying to lose body fat. Thanks!

### Shijin13 — 2012-03-16T17:05:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-1484>

When I LHT or HIIT I do it right before my next meal. I eat keto. In the new monster thread Quelsen gives an excellent explanation why you don't need this if you are keto adapted

No need to eat lots of carbs when you are a fat burner.

### PaleoDentist — 2012-03-17T03:24:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-1828>

OK, but how how does the glycogen get replaced when eating low carb? I thought it was important to replenish glycogen stores by eating carbs after a workout...

### Glamorama — 2012-03-17T04:54:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-1860>

> **PaleoDentist;1831 wrote:** OK, but how how does the glycogen get replaced when eating low carb? I thought it was important to replenish glycogen stores by eating carbs after a workout...

Because I'm in early fall here in Oz, I still have 30-60 gram carbs daily. 14 grams of those come from a can of pure coconut water with pulp straight after my LHT-sessions. From my understanding, you can build muscle with just fat, protein and CT... My muscle is coming out nicely with strength-increase every week, and I'm low-carb, but not keto yet, as I'm waiting till winter.

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-03-17T13:44:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-1918>

If you are eating a keto - paleo diet but still maintain a carb intake (from vegetables) and you keep it in the under 50 gram a day range, you will never deplete your glycogen stores. The avg human can store 400 grams of gylcogen in their muscles before it gets stored as fat.

The trick is by eating keto you turn yourself into a fat burner instead of a sugar burner. Your body will use fat as fuel source saving muscle and liver glycogen for when it is needed in emergency situations or extended bouts of stress.

The only time you completely deplete them is through intense exercise over a long duration( marathons) or through a diet that is completely void of carbs (Eskimos eating blubber and meat)

Meal timing is important for muscles. So after a workout consume a protein and carb meal. Think 30 grams protein and 15 -20 grams of carbs from veggie sources. Keep your fat low in this meal so you do not overly slow absorption (the protein will slow it down enough) Spread the rest of your carb intake over your other meals.

The reason to put the two together is glycogen helps shuttle amino acids into muscle for recovery and repair.

BAB- protein/fat little or no carbs

PWO meal- protein/carb little or no fat

Dinner (if neccessary) protein/fat little or no carb

adjust timing depending on when you wotkout.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-03-17T14:33:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-1935>

> **PaleoDentist;1831 wrote:** OK, but how how does the glycogen get replaced when eating low carb? I thought it was important to replenish glycogen stores by eating carbs after a workout...

your liver does it......gluconeogenesis. IF you decided to be warm adapted you will need the carbs.......if you decide to be cold adapted you do not need them much at all. It really comes down to how you want to live.

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-03-17T15:45:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-1962>

> **Jack Kruse;1940 wrote:** your liver does it......gluconeogenesis. IF you decided to be warm adapted you will need the carbs.......if you decide to be cold adapted you do not need them much at all. It really comes down to how you want to live.

Dr. Kruse, just so I understand. It was my understanding that gluconeogenesis does not occur until you have depleted all of your muscle and liver glycogen. Through extended intense exercise, bouts of starvation or a diet void of a carbohydrate source. (Inuits)

If I understand you correctly, you are saying that once you become cold adapted the pathway bypasses what you have stored in muscle and liver and that gluconeogenesis (the making of glucose from non-carbohydrates) occurs in the liver. So it fills the storage of liver and muscle and uses the leftovers to fuel the body along with keytone bodies from a ketopaleo diet.

I will have to reread the CT series to make sure I understand this action.

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-03-17T20:48:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2096>

> **Continuum Fitness;1967 wrote:** Dr. Kruse, just so I understand. It was my understanding that gluconeogenesis does not occur until you have depleted all of your muscle and liver glycogen. Through extended intense exercise, bouts of starvation or a diet void of a carbohydrate source. (Inuits)
>
>
>
> If I understand you correctly, you are saying that once you become cold adapted the pathway bypasses what you have stored in muscle and liver and that gluconeogenesis (the making of glucose from non-carbohydrates) occurs in the liver. So it fills the storage of liver and muscle and uses the leftovers to fuel the body along with keytone bodies from a ketopaleo diet.
>
>
>
> I will have to reread the CT series to make sure I understand this action.

Thanks for this thread. This is really helpful. And my take is that to the degree that you are cold adapted is the degree to which you can do without carbs post exercise. IMHO which is very, very H, it would be possible to have a false sense of how LS you've become other than relative to your LR experience. (unless you test!!) Ditto for how cold adapted one is. So which is the worse error - eating too many carbs post WO because you are more cold adapted than you realized? (Banana in Alaska winter problem) OR eating too few carbs thinking you are cold adapted when you aren't really there yet?

### SimonM — 2012-03-17T22:54:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2126>

Continuing misunderstandings here

There are no Inuit sprinters/middle distance runners for a very good reason. And don't tell me they CAN sprint to get away from polar bears.

Hunter-gatherers of any stripe, even those who are persistence hunters, on average do not run all that fast. Many times much slower than even an average calibre modern marathon runner, but they can keep going ALL day. (Modern runners find it very difficult to keep up with traditional persistence hunters because modern runners are used to running in straight lines on even terrain and are not used to ducking under trees and jumping snakes etc - it is not a speed thing).

The keto/ low-no carb plans is great for endurance and abysmal for speed or speed/endurance. During any activity with a wodge of anaerobic quality to it you will fail big-time without carbs. The reason is that even if you are a fat-burning machine (and many successful ultra endurance athletes like the long course and Ironman triathlon specialists have achieved this - it is a routine effect of typical base building), during a highly anaerobic event, the body cannot produce glucose from fat fast enough. That is why studies have shown big gains in endurance with loss of speed in athletes switching to fat-dominant diets.

Yes, you can see huge gains in V02 max from becoming a fat-burning machine, but it case this gets tossed back at me, v02max is a very poor predictor of competitive performance. Other facts such as economy are more important. In other words, having a high V02max does not necessarily mean you can out-perform anybody with a lower Vo2max. As Prof Tim Noakes has pointed out - if that were true then all competitions would be decided in the lab.

Recently some elites have been playing around with a "train low, race high" thing, where they train on a high fat diet (and feel absolutely crap) and then race on high carbs, and it seems promising. Arguably the world's #1 endurance guy ultraman world champion Jonas Colting eats low-carbish Paleo in training and scarfs high carb, even junk high-carb, while competing.

Carbs for higher intensity exercise are absolutely essential: Armstrong (cold adapted?) 70% carbs; Phelps (cold adapted?) massive amounts of carbs; Sherpas (cold adapted) eg Apa Sherpa - record holder with 22 Everest summits - high carbs, and high carbs + high fat once working past base camp; Kenyans (world's dominant runners above sprint distances) - high carb.

Dr Kruse makes the valid point that the longevity of high-carbers like the Kenyans is probably not good, and I have lost track of the number of former Olympic-level athletes who are suffering in later years with very serious health conditions...so it may be, as Dr K says - that there is a trade-off here - short-term glory (on carbs) or long-term health.

So, @cgk, when you ask

"So which is the worse error - eating too many carbs post WO because you are more cold adapted than you realized? (Banana in Alaska winter problem) OR eating too few carbs thinking you are cold adapted when you aren't really there yet?"

the answer probably is...it depends whether quick recovery and ability to do the training necessary to develop anaerobic capacity and power is your aim, of whether you are more focused on health and longevity and the ability to sustain a lower-level performance over 4 hours +.

Great thread! Thanks guys.

### Barry — 2012-03-18T01:56:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2182>

Simon & co

some very good points there, this is certainly a big topic though and its one I have a fair bit of experience with. I'll outline a few things here, theory, hypothesis and practical experience. Need Dr K's input and then we'll see where we go from there

I'll start from the top and work backwards:

For about 3 years I have been keto adapting. Its been a gradual process of increasing fat intake, cycling carb intake, and gradually reducing carb intake. I've aided the adaptation through my training strategies. Basically, I;ve done a lot (and I mean a lot) of fasted state training. When I say "training"... I mean ultra endurance stuff.. Ironman type training... 4-5hr bike rides, 3-4hr runs (and longer but I'll get to that) and long swims. I built this up over the years from doing say a 1hr run fasted to a 5hr bike ride fasted, with minimal eating during too. I now do all my morning training (5-6days a week) fasted, i.e. no breakfast. It could be a 2-3K swim in the pool or a 5hr bike ride. Always fasted, coffee being my breakfast of choice ;-). All this was done based on the research I've studied on glycogen depleted training, PPAR, CPT-1, IMTG, Mitchodrial Biogensis, IL-6 and basically everything related to upregulating fat adaptation.

I now run ultramarathons and have competed in 100mile mountain races. I've raced in 8 ultramarathons over the past 2 years, distances between 30 and 100miles, all trail mountain type. I raced each of them fasted. That is, I get up, while everyone else is shovelling porridge, banana's, bagels etc into them to "fuel"... I am sipping on a fresh coffee, and maybe one or two ergogenic aids. I've won 2 races, finished 2nd twice, 3rd in another and been in the top 10 in the remainder. I mention this not to "bragg" but this to put things in context in terms of the level I am competing at.

Theory and Hypothesis

This is where I am hoping I can link in with Dr K. So the theory is all based on fat adaptation and fuel efficiency. So upregulate PPAR, switch on beta oxidation, improve CPT-1 function and various other enzymes and transporters. For more info on this, I've written an article which explains most things: [http://www.optimumnutrition4sport.com/?page\_id=547](http://http://www.optimumnutrition4sport.com/?page_id=547) ... looking back on it, I have missed out on a couple of things which I;ve read through Dr. K's work. The effect IL-6 has (and I mean good affect in terms of lipolysis), UCP3 and possibly 1 or 2 other mechanisms.

The Link/Hypothesis:

My speculation (and it is just a speculation which is why I am sharing this here) is the adaptations gained from CT in relation to ketogensis can be also gained through ultra fasted state endurance training.

\- it improves insulin sensitivity and thus leptin sensitivity

\- it increases fat oxidation both adipose and IMTG

\- it increases mitochondrial biogensis

\- FAT/CD36 is upregulated

\- NPY is swtiched off during exercise

\- UCP3 is upregulated

\- other pathways that i have forgotten !

There are studies on athletes which support the above, mostly. The studies show that the adaptations take place but that performance doesn't improve in most cases. The reason for this is quite simple. As Dr.K mentioned, it takes time to fully adapt 24-36months. Most of the studies are carried out between 4-8weeks. On top of that they are neither CT adapted, keto or in my case "fasted state" adapted.

I'm probably leaving a few things out as my head has literally been melting since I started reading Dr K's blogs !! but I hope he can comment on the above !

Another Link:

Just to put things in a bit more perspective. I have run over hills and mountains for 6-7hrs, at a decent pace, with some big max heart rate climbs and efforts, on empty - no breakfast, and with only 2 snacks during - I use coconut/nut butter/oat homemade bars. So we're talking maybe 40-50g of carbs for the entire run. I know there are going to be some serious concerns about cortisol and telomeres etc but I don't want to conversation to center around that for now. I do what I do because I love it. Its an amazing feeling and experience. And with that I think comes some big time epigenetics. There has got to be some gene expressions as a result of this possibly relating to SIRT-1 and PDC-1 amongst others. Again, hopefully Dr K can comment on this !

Grey Area

As mentioned I think by Simon, the big grey area for me is top end intensity training, so flat out intervals whether that be 600-1200m reps on the track or 2-3min efforts on the bike or even just threshold work. If we are in the anaerobic state, we need glucose. Now the thing is, there are other systems - Cori Cycle and Glucose Alanine Cycle. Possibly also so glycolytic amino acids can work too.

Apart from my own athletic adventures, I work with elite athletes too - Tour de France Cyclists, Olympic Triathletes and World Class Distance Runners. From my experience in this field, I can tell you that I know of none that are following a ketogenic diet. Some of my atheletes are close, but nowhere near

### SimonM — 2012-03-18T02:56:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2214>

Great stuff Barry!

Just to reiterate, when you say

> ...there are other systems - Cori Cycle and Glucose Alanine Cycle. Possibly also so glycolytic amino acids can work too.

the problem as I understand it is that these are just not fast enough to fuel sustained high intensity/speed efforts.

I am a masters world champion (5m x-country), but my current emphasis/obsession is running a superfast track mile, so like you I am in a highly practical n=1 experiment where feedback on fuel/training choices is relatively obvious.

Thanks for opening up on here! Look forward to continuing conversations.

### SimonM — 2012-03-18T02:58:05+0200

### Barry — 2012-03-18T20:25:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2438>

Simon

yes, celebrated in keto style ;-)

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-03-18T22:48:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2463>

Hi guys, I want to jump in this thread as I am looking for performance gains, though not quite in the same vein.

I am 40 yo female powerlifter, I have been using CT going on 3 weeks now. Cold baths, 55F for 35-60min, almost daily in the evening after I train and have dinner, and 5 min cold showers 55Falso, in the morning.

I have seen some promising results, but it is too soon to be sure. Subjectively, I feel less sore, and have greater energy. I feel recovered from training sooner, like I could handle an extra day's training, but I won't do that just yet. I have hit some PRs on body weight movements, like pull ups, and I have hit some recent PRs in squat and DL, but I switched programming (and coaches) so I can't truly say my recent improvements are direct result of CT. It sure feels like it though!

I have a meet in 4 weeks. I will not do anything different from what I am doing now, eating VLC, about 120g of protein, baths in evening, random spot icing and the AM showers.

I'm thinking of trying CT before training, or even crazier, during. As in, when I am done with squatting, icing my glutes hammies while I bench. Then when I move onto deadlifts or powercleans, icing, chest, delts. Or icing before my lifts. Yeah, nutty. Which is why I won't do it until after the meet.

What forms of CT are you using regularly, and when?

### SimonM — 2012-03-18T23:18:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2470>

Hey Barry,

Interesting. My figures put the cost much higher. I am running sub-5-minute pace = minimum 11kcal from carbs per minute required, which I \*think\* means that I am looking for around 66g of glucose to cover the race itself (assuming it lasts 5 minutes). Then I also have to cover the energy cost of the warm-up - some of which is intensive - not to mention travel, not to mention just getting out of the house! OK, some of that is going to be fuelled by fat, so I don't want to exaggerate, and we also need to bear in mind that all activities use all the energy pathways, there is no clear delineation. Also, I am rating the mile effort as 50/50 aerobic anaerobic.

Whatever...you make a good point. I'd never done the sums before, but it does validate that eating low carb (ie

### SimonM — 2012-03-18T23:27:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2476>

Hi Teenie Leek,

> I have seen some promising results, but it is too soon to be sure. Subjectively, I feel less sore, and have greater energy. I feel recovered from training sooner, like I could handle an extra day's training, but I won't do that just yet.

I think this is where the effects of CT become really obvious  Elites have been using ice-cold baths (usually just for the legs) for at least 20 for exactly those reasons. The benefits are even more obvious if you \*have\* to train 2 or 3 times a day. In which case it becomes essential. This puts CT on a par with steroids: better recovery = more and higher quality training possible = improved performance. If you combine CT with low-carb, which Jack says is what works and what you are doing, then Jack seems to say you will access that "superpower" zone. It will be great to hear what happens when your other variables have evened out.

Did you see Jack's post/blog where he mentioned the Vasper training system? [http://www.vasper.com/](http://http://www.vasper.com/) This is the origin of "Freeze and Squeeze" - I'd love to know what results you could get doing that!

### Barry — 2012-03-19T01:02:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2500>

> **Teenie Leek;2470 wrote:** Hi guys, I want to jump in this thread as I am looking for performance gains, though not quite in the same vein.
>
>
>
>
>
> What forms of CT are you using regularly, and when?

Hi Teenie Leek (sounds a bit funny saying this, anyway)

CT I've done has not been specific in terms of Dr K's methods, more just training outdoors for hours in minus temperatures.

In terms of timing, most say post CT is best time to train.

I think we do all have to bear one thing in mind. Over recent years, ice baths etc have been shown to blunt adaptation to the muscle stress. So while it does numb the pain when used post heavy training, it can actually inhibit the beneficial inflammatory response

So my advice to you would be to CT pre workouts, not during training or not post training. However, during competition, this is where CT post is fine and even during, albeit this is something I have never really seen or have experience with

hope that helps

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-03-19T01:21:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2505>

Simon, I did look through the Vesper site, and have a few ideas: sleeping on a conductive mattress pad would be a start for the grounding. As far as compression and cooling, I was also thinking about training while wearing a cooling vest. The compression part is a bit more difficult. Powerlifters can lift massive amounts with compression clothing, squat suits and bench shirts. I compete "raw" which is just belt and knee wraps, when one competes with compression suits/shirts, that is "geared" and a different category. I cannot wear compression gear in competition. Just cotton tshirt, lycra singlet. I don't want to train in anything that I won't be able to compete in.

Now you have me thinking though, I could train my volume days in semi-compression (under armour) and do my intensity days (which mimic the meet, 3 attempts at the 3 lifts, low volume but very heavy) in my regular gear. hmmmmph. lots to consider

I work 2 jobs, and I have tried to do 6 days a week, experimented with Bulgarian/Romanian methods, and was too much with my work schedule. After the meet, I may add in a 4th day, on the weekend of "accessory" work: farmer's walks, tire flips, weighted pull ups and dips. I'm still in the idea stages.

My XH spent a summer in Scotland years ago, came back with a few new expressions that charmed my ears

I seem to remember for the Summer Olympics in Athens that the US marathoners wore cooling vests and stayed indoors in the air conditioning right up to the last minute, while other athletes were outside "acclimitzing" to the heat that they had to run in. I remember reading that in Runner's World (I used to run marathons, and followed the US racing scene for a bit) Also that Deena Kastor and Meb Keflezghi used ice baths after all workouts. I used to jump in my unheated swimming pool (65-70F) after long runs (15+ miles) with seemingly no ill effect. I wasn't competitive, I just did them to hide an eating disorder, but that's another story for another day.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-03-19T02:42:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2535>

> **Continuum Fitness;1967 wrote:** Dr. Kruse, just so I understand. It was my understanding that gluconeogenesis does not occur until you have depleted all of your muscle and liver glycogen. Through extended intense exercise, bouts of starvation or a diet void of a carbohydrate source. (Inuits)
>
>
>
> If I understand you correctly, you are saying that once you become cold adapted the pathway bypasses what you have stored in muscle and liver and that gluconeogenesis (the making of glucose from non-carbohydrates) occurs in the liver. So it fills the storage of liver and muscle and uses the leftovers to fuel the body along with keytone bodies from a ketopaleo diet.
>
>
>
> I will have to reread the CT series to make sure I understand this action.

When you are cold adapted you burn straight fat and you make any glucose you need from TG's......you become super efficient but as I said the performance part elite athletes want comes at 24-36 months. Now some of the best athletes in the world are using CT. And they are pushing the boundaries.

### SimonM — 2012-03-19T05:20:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2605>

> Now you have me thinking though, I could train my volume days in semi-compression (under armour) and do my intensity days (which mimic the meet, 3 attempts at the 3 lifts, low volume but very heavy) in my regular gear. hmmmmph. lots to consider

One quick point - what you are calling "compression" and what Vesper calls "compression" are apples and oranges. From what I understand from the website, the Vesper wraps are more like tourniquets - much greater force being applied to actively (almost) block blood flow and INCREASE build up of lactate.

### SimonM — 2012-03-19T05:34:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2617>

> **Barry;2501 wrote:** Simon - @11kcal/min for 5mins, thats 55kcal which = 13.75g of CHO !! not sure where you got the 66g ?

Maths is not my strong point, but according to the calculators I've found online you are using a kcal per minute of running at 9 or 10:00 per mile pace and a guesstimate at my weight (I guess). Obviously, I am running sub-5:00 per mile pace - kcal cost is way higher - and I am 149lb.

I'm prepared to put in the year or two Jack says are necessary to adapt to CR and keto, but as I've said in previous posts, my hesitancy is due to the fact that there are no examples of speed athletes having any success. Jack continues to mention elite athletes using CR, but I guess because of confidentiality cannot mention names. Meanwhile, all the high performers he has mentioned as being prime examples of what can be achieved - Armstrong, Phelps and the Sherpas - all eat high-carb.

If you'll excuse me banging on about this - it's not a question of whether or not we can do short intervals, or high intensity workouts on keto or low carb (with or without CT), as clearly we can...it's a question of the quality/speed of those intervals. I'd like to see some records/ results/examples that show that world-class performances are possible.

Cheers Barry.

Dr K - some help here??????

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-03-19T18:45:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2798>

Thanks both Simon and Barry for your comments. I got into all of this because I was a 47 year old crossfitter suffering from crappy sleep, lousy recovery, and ultimately was trading DOMS for same day pain - even though I was eating 90% + paleo/primal. Performance is on my radar screen, but functionality/longevity has to come first - that was a promise I made when I became a parent right? IOTW, I don't really care if I have to scale a WOD or do a different WOD altogether. I just want to be doing the RIGHT WOD and eating the RIGHT food to go with it.

I need a graphic that somehow plots anaerobic/aerobic exercise against duration against temperature/cold adaptation and is then somehow superimposes what type of pathway the body is in and then how you would best fuel before, during, after the exercise.

Some sort of x, y, z axis, color and pattern coded gem that would make it easier to figure this out.

Just another N=1 who wants to eat and move to be well for a good long time.

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-03-19T18:50:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2803>

> **SimonM;2612 wrote:** One quick point - what you are calling "compression" and what Vesper calls "compression" are apples and oranges. From what I understand from the website, the Vesper wraps are more like tourniquets - much greater force being applied to actively (almost) block blood flow and INCREASE build up of lactate.

Try putting on a squat suit. It is not under armour like material. You struggle and sweat getting that thing on. It constricts like mad. It IS like a tourniquet.

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-03-19T19:01:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2810>

> **cgk;2807 wrote:** I just want to be doing the RIGHT WOD and eating the RIGHT food to go with it.
>
>
>
> Just another N=1 who wants to eat and move to be well for a good long time.

Does your CF box offer strength only classes, or the option to do strength only portion of a class, and opt out of the metcon? Though I realize that many CF boxes just offer metcons, there are a few like mine, that are strength biased and have lots of strength only workouts and/or strength followed by short conditioning. We also have lifting only classes where they can follow a custom program we've written, or one they have come up with. Another idea is scale workouts yourself, telling the coach what you plan on doing, rather than doing "their" version of scaled. Generally I'd have people scale reps and rounds and/or time limit on an amrap. If people have health issues, I generally scale all 3, or just give another workout altogether.

Your coaches should be amenable to your requests. If they aren't, I'd suggest finding a new box, or following another box's programming from home. HTH

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-03-19T19:22:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2824>

Teenie Leek

Definitely! Seems that the pure strength days/ metcon days in our box- are less obvious than they used to be. It is definitely harder to program/plan yourself, when WOD's are posted the night before. Totally concur that you can program yourself within a box, but there are limits to how much you can do that and still feel like a member of the box. During the Leptin Rx, I just went in and did one of KStarr's MWOD's. Now it is time to figure out what next... I would just as soon do Oly lifts 2 days a week and 2 body weight metcons and hope that is enough and not too much. No clue yet on timing either... since I did an 0515 class before breakfast until I started the leptin reset. I guess working out fasted before lunch and then eating x or y for lunch based on whether or not it was a strength or a metcon would be one way to go about it.

CGK,

my only advice would be this - don't follow any x,y,z axis chart with fancy colours and patterns !! Use the key principles related to health and fitness - keto/paleo template, low stress, good sleep, natural movement/strength based exercise etc... then go on feel and listen to your own body ! it knows best... no PT or WOD chart is going to know how you feel and think...

Teenie Leek's advice is good... partition your strength/HIIT sessions based on feel.

But like I said, above all, be good to your body and it will return the favour !

Barry

### SimonM — 2012-03-19T21:49:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-2917>

Yeah what he said

No, but really,as my coaching mentor Ian King has said, sports scientists are actually sports historians... they are so far behind that you won't find a graphic that is up to date, let alone can stay up to date. Besides which, thanks to Jack, we are doing stuff (CR) that no one else has looked at it yet, and as you can see from the discussions here, we are still trying to work out what the effects are on energy systems and how to fuel and so on.

The other thing is that the "right" training for you depends totally on your long-term goals, which you've clearly stated, and on breaking them down into specific attributes, or qualities, that you want to develop. So you are prioritising longevity - what does that look like for you, and what do you need to achieve it? Similarly, "functionality" - an exercise isn't functional because some functional fitness guru says it is - it is functional if it fits your unique needs/goals. So functional for what, excactly?

The only thing I would add to Barry's comment is that in the "listen to your body" category, pay equal or more attention to your performance figures - that is, record and chart your performances. I have often had brilliant sessions when, going in, I have "felt" like crap. I set my 400m PR on a day when I had been physically sick a couple of hours before. If you are obsessive enough to also keep some brief record of your food choices and timing, plus weather (temp and pressure), then you may start spotting some patterns.

### Barry — 2012-03-19T22:20:43+0200

Simon

I'm with you here in terms of elite athletes and performance measures. I do think that you are possibly creating a psychological barrier in thinking that it is not possible or hasn't been done before. Remember Roger Bannister ?? Like everything, you must believe it is possible in order for it to happen.

But without getting into a philosophical debate, I think speed energy production is possible through low carb. As I said, you don't need much to run 100m or even 1 mile efforts. And of course you haver your PCr system as well as the others I've mentioned, in addition to the ones we may not know about yet ! And yes, fat burning needs oxygen... but PCr, Cori and Glucose/Alanine doesn't !

Re CGK advice - very good points, especially related to tracking your own performance, good/bad days etc. Oh, we're not all "sports historians" though ;-)

### SimonM — 2012-03-21T02:11:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-3660>

>

>

>
> Re CGK advice - very good points, especially related to tracking your own performance, good/bad days etc. Oh, we're not all "sports historians" though ;-)

Interesting point, Barry. Bannister was not only medically trained but was researching 02 uptake by breaking down runners on a treadmill - and this in the 1950s! He had the advantage over everyone else chasing the sub-4 mile in that he knew from his experiments that 3 x 400 in 60sec + 1 x 400 in 59 WAS physiologically possible. So I'm looking for pre-attempt data, too

And I have a wonderful anaerobic energy source in lactate (which I train to use) thanks very much, without all your other esoteric and/or unknown pathways!!!! hahahahahahaha

I was looking on some of the low-carb forums to see what others' experience was. One guy, a cop doing SWAT team training, was adamant that going Very Low Carb had no negative effects whatsoever on his "high intensity" training. The basic plank of his HIT was running 1.5 miles in just under 12 minutes! Yes quite. I train with 60-year-old+ women who can do repeat miles at that pace. Another guy agreed that VLC was the way to go for HIT: he runs marathons. So just for the record, what I'm looking at is the optimal fuel for repeats at a minimum of 90 per cent VO2 max.

Interestingly, one guy said he'd had a intensity performance fall-off when he went low-carb, and had "cured" it by adding in more fat - to the extent of "snacking" on tablespoonfuls of oil. (MCTs anyone?) You mentioned you were eating loads of fat. Maybe that is a missing factor for me - get fat intake to >40 per cent? Any thoughts?

### malinamartis — 2012-03-24T11:11:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/post-workout-carb-eating.205/post-5353>

But i must suggest you should consume the food which do not have any kind of of fat oil, this may be lead to increase bad over muscles on your body. So avoid eating more oily food this can also helps in your weight control too.

[kamagra](http://http://www.astermeds.com/buy-kamagra-online.aspx)

# Thread: CT ( non-dieoff) side effects
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### meyoolia@gmail.com — 2012-03-16T17:09:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-1485>

Hi all,

I'm tubbing daily for just over a week - up to 40 minutes - and the last few days I'm noticing persistently cold hands and/or feet (sometimes tingly toes, too), even before I've CT'd for that day. Hopefully this is just a transitional thing, but it's a bit of a bother! Input?

### Croak — 2012-03-16T17:13:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-1488>

Body's response to lowered core temp is to reduce blood flow to the extremities. Might be overdoing the time or temps and getting your core too cold. Try to keep hands/feet out of the water and see if that helps any too.

### MartiD — 2012-03-16T17:18:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-1492>

I have to keep my feet and hands out or I really tremble while in tub. I find it much easier to really relax if they are out. Good luck and keep us posted. FYI...I am doing two a day, the one in the morning is only about 15-20 min and evening is more like 30. I'm not patient enough to do 40!

### meyoolia@gmail.com — 2012-03-16T19:04:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-1592>

> **Croak;1490 wrote:** Body's response to lowered core temp is to reduce blood flow to the extremities. Might be overdoing the time or temps and getting your core too cold. Try to keep hands/feet out of the water and see if that helps any too.

I actually \*do\* keep hands/feet out of the tub. I usually CT in the later morning....and for the past couple of days I've been noticing cold feet (mostly left one, go figure) anytime, even now, pre-tub. Considering putting on a thick pair of socks once I'm in, so they stay dry'ish.

### JC2K — 2012-03-16T19:38:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-1615>

I use neoprene booties and gloves from my scuba gear. Makes life substantially easier, especially with the feet. I had real problems with cold without them.

### Lyndra — 2012-03-17T01:12:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-1760>

> **JC2K;1617 wrote:** I use neoprene booties and gloves from my scuba gear. Makes life substantially easier, especially with the feet. I had real problems with cold without them.

Same for me. One time I tried wearing neoprene shoes instead of booties. My feet were miserable in the tub and I shook violently for a couple hours after I got out. Now it's neoprene booties plus gloves and a cap.

### JC2K — 2012-03-17T01:28:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-1767>

I haven't needed the cap yet (also have a neoprene beanie for scuba  ), but my water's not in the 50 degree range yet. Got to get a bunch of ice for that I think.

### meyoolia@gmail.com — 2012-03-17T01:59:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-1783>

> **August;1775 wrote:** Stupid question: What's dieing off? I thought "die off effect" usually referred to Candida . . . . I think with CT the hope is that some subcutaneous fat cells will come to a bad end . . .

oops! my mistake. I should have phrased it as "detox". I've had candida issues for YEARS, so that's what my fingers threw in.

### meyoolia@gmail.com — 2012-03-17T19:55:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-2076>

> **meyoolia;1487 wrote:** Hi all,
>
> I'm tubbing daily for just over a week - up to 40 minutes - and the last few days I'm noticing persistently cold hands and/or feet (sometimes tingly toes, too), even before I've CT'd for that day. Hopefully this is just a transitional thing, but it's a bit of a bother! Input?

and to add to this, I've finally figured out that the nasty scabby-itchies on parts of my scalp are likely contact dermatitis from the (neoprene?) of my cpap sleep mask headgear. I've been wearing it for 3 years and \*now\* it decides to give me an allergic reaction?? yeeshk. Changes, they are afoot! guess I'll have to figure out how to stitch up a flannel sleeve thingy for it til this sensitivity passes  .... and I've also upped my b complex and C's... \*crossed fingers\*

### meyoolia@gmail.com — 2012-03-17T19:57:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-2077>
